    16→- ✓ Detailed, phased implementation plan refined from specification into actionable phases — v1.0
    17→- ✓ Architecture documentation covering recursive loop, hypernetwork, evolution operator, adapter serving — v1.0
    18→- ✓ Repo restructured to match implementation plan component layout — v2.0
    19→- ✓ All template placeholders removed, services renamed and scaffolded — v2.0
    20→- ✓ Quality gates pass: pytest, ruff, GPU import scan, 501 stubs, docker-compose, mkdocs — v2.0
    21→- ✓ Scientific article documenting theoretical foundation (abstract, background, methods, results, discussion) — v3.0
    22→- ✓ Complete API wireframes for all 11 components with Google-style docstrings — v4.0
    23→- ✓ Every public method/function raises NotImplementedError with descriptive messages — v4.0
    24→- ✓ Failing tests for every public method (TDD red phase: 87 tests, 13 expected failures) — v4.0
    25→- ✓ Shared test fixture factories (conftest.py) for DRY test setup across components — v4.0
    26→- ✓ Adapter Registry CRUD with SQLite persistence, WAL mode, concurrent access — v5.0
    27→- ✓ Provider-agnostic inference (InferenceProvider ABC with vLLM + Ollama backends) — v5.0
    28→- ✓ Agent loop: generate → execute → reflect → save_trajectory with LangGraph routing — v5.0
    29→- ✓ Trajectory persistence (record, load, format_for_sft) — v5.0
    30→- ✓ QLoRA training pipeline (trajectory → SFT → PEFT train → safetensors → registry) — v5.0
    31→- ✓ Doc-to-LoRA hypernetwork (Perceiver-based single-pass adapter generation) — v5.0
    32→- ✓ HumanEval kill-switch gate (Pass@k metrics, 5% improvement threshold) — v5.0
    33→- ✓ Training HTTP dispatch (POST /train/lora, POST /train/hypernetwork with background jobs) — v5.0
    34→- ✓ GPU import deferral pattern (all GPU deps inside function bodies for CPU-only CI) — v5.0
    35→- ✓ lora-server with vLLM base image and runtime LoRA configuration — v5.0
    36→
    37→### Active
    38→
    39→- [ ] Hardware and environment validation (dual RTX 4090, CUDA, PyTorch, vLLM live test)
    40→- [ ] Core hypothesis validation on real hardware (kill-switch gate end-to-end with actual model)
    41→
    42→### Out of Scope
    43→
    44→- Mobile app — local-first desktop/server only
    45→- Cloud API dependencies for inference — must run entirely on local hardware
    46→- Tensor parallelism — confirmed vLLM bug #21471 with TP+LoRA on consumer GPUs; PP=2 required
    47→- Frontend beyond basic HITL UI — focus is on agent backend infrastructure
    48→- evolution-svc — depends on populated adapter library from multiple sessions; v6+
    49→- Adapter merging into base weights — loses hot-swap composability; vLLM Punica kernels handle LoRA with near-zero overhead
    50→- Concurrent training + inference — CUDA OOM; must schedule sequentially
    51→
    52→## Context
    53→
    54→**Shipped through v5.0.** Monorepo with 7,834 lines of Python across 11 components (6 libraries + 5 services). Full working system: adapter registry → inference providers → agent loop → QLoRA training → Doc-to-LoRA hypernetwork → kill-switch gate. 151 tests passing, mypy strict, ruff clean.
    55→
    56→**Tech stack:** Python 3.12+, FastAPI, LangGraph, SQLModel/SQLAlchemy, Pydantic, uv workspace, MkDocs with MathJax, PEFT, safetensors.
    57→
    58→**Hardware target:** Dual RTX 4090 (24GB each, 48GB total VRAM) with CXL interconnect, AMD Threadripper 7960X. QLoRA required for 24GB per-GPU constraint. Pipeline parallelism (PP=2, TP=1).
    59→
    60→**Research basis:** Doc-to-LoRA (arXiv:2602.15902), S-LoRA (arXiv:2311.03285), QLoRA (arXiv:2305.14314), PBB (arXiv:2506.18777).
    61→
    62→**Known tech debt from v5.0:** api-service adapter/session routes return 501 (HTTP exposure deferred); training-svc not in docker-compose; kill-switch gate not wired into training completion path; get_provider_for_step() no production caller; 4 evaluation stub functions for evolution-svc.
    63→
    64→## Constraints
    65→
    66→- **Local-first**: Must run entirely on local hardware. No cloud API dependencies for inference.
    67→- **Hardware**: Dual RTX 4090 + CXL + Threadripper 7960X (48GB VRAM total)
    68→- **Monorepo**: Build on existing template structure (uv workspace, FastAPI)
    69→- **Python**: Primary implementation language, using uv for package management
    70→
    71→## Key Decisions
    72→
    73→| Decision | Rationale | Outcome |
    74→|----------|-----------|---------|
    75→| Build on existing monorepo template | Reuse FastAPI/LangGraph infrastructure | ✓ Good (v2.0) |
    76→| Dual RTX 4090 + CXL as target hardware | 48GB VRAM total, QLoRA required for 24GB per-GPU | — Pending |
    77→| Doc-to-LoRA hypernetwork approach | Instant adapter generation (<1s) vs minutes for fine-tuning | ✓ Good (v5.0) — Perceiver architecture implemented, kill-switch gate built |
    78→| Documentation-first milestone | Validate approach through thorough planning before implementation | ✓ Good (v1.0) |
    79→| Restructure before implement | Align monorepo layout to implementation plan components | ✓ Good (v2.0) |
    80→| Scientific article before implementation | Sharpen algorithm design before coding | ✓ Good (v3.0) |
    81→| TDD wireframes before implementation | Red-phase tests define expected behavior before writing logic | ✓ Good (v4.0) |
    82→| Factory fixtures as test-only (not runtime) | Avoid runtime factory pattern complexity | ✓ Good (v4.0) |
    83→| Centralized ruff config in root pyproject.toml | Per-component configs broke with dotted-key format | ✓ Good (v4.0) |
    84→| lora-server as Dockerfile-only (not uv workspace) | GPU-dependent service doesn't fit CPU-only workspace | ✓ Good (v2.0) |
    85→| Engine from sqlalchemy.engine not sqlmodel | SQLModel does not re-export Engine | ✓ Good (v5.0) |
    86→| GPU imports deferred inside function bodies | CPU-only CI compatibility without conditional install | ✓ Good (v5.0) |
    87→| sys.modules injection for GPU dep mocking | unittest.mock.patch fails when package not installed | ✓ Good (v5.0) |
    88→| bfloat16 hardcoded in QLoRA config | float16 causes silent NaN loss at 7B scale | ✓ Good (v5.0) |
    89→| No modules_to_save in LoraConfig | vLLM rejects embed_tokens/lm_head in adapter artifacts | ✓ Good (v5.0) |
    90→| _LazyHypernetworkProxy for DocToLoraHypernetwork | Keeps module importable without torch installed | ✓ Good (v5.0) |
    91→
    92→---
    93→*Last updated: 2026-03-06 after v5.0 milestone shipped*
    94→

   140→    task_description: str = "Write an add function",
   141→) -> dict[str, Any]:

   162→    # assistant content should be from the last step with tests_passed=True

   181→    result = format_for_sft(trajectory)

1→"""Tests for model_training.trainer module."""

     3→import json

    12→def _make_trajectory(tmp_path: Path, session_id: str, outcome: str) -> Path:
    13→    """Write a trajectory JSON file to tmp_path and return its path."""
    14→    traj = {
    15→        "session_id": session_id,
    16→        "task_description": "Write a hello world function",
    17→        "task_type": "code-gen",
    18→        "adapter_ids": [],
    19→        "outcome": outcome,
    20→        "timestamp": "2026-03-05T00:00:00Z",
    21→        "steps": [
    22→            {
    23→                "attempt": 1,
    24→                "generated_code": "def hello(): return 'hello'",
    25→                "tests_passed": True,
    26→            }
    27→        ],
    28→    }
    29→    traj_file = tmp_path / f"{session_id}.json"
    30→    traj_file.write_text(json.dumps(traj))
    31→    return traj_file
    32→
    33→
    34→def test_train_qlora_function_importable_without_gpu() -> None:
    35→    """train_qlora is importable without GPU libs (all GPU imports are deferred)."""
    36→    from model_training.trainer import train_qlora  # noqa: F401
    37→
    38→    assert callable(train_qlora)
    39→
    40→
    41→def test_train_qlora_rejects_missing_trajectory(
    42→    tmp_path: Path, monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch
    43→) -> None:

    45→    monkeypatch.setenv("RUNE_TRAJECTORY_DIR", str(tmp_path))
    46→
    47→    # Inject fake GPU modules so deferred imports succeed without real GPU libs
    48→    _inject_fake_gpu_modules()

    50→        from model_training.trainer import train_qlora
    51→
    52→        with pytest.raises(FileNotFoundError):

    83→@pytest.mark.xfail(reason="Full GPU mocking chain is fragile in CPU CI", strict=False)
    84→def test_train_and_register_creates_adapter_dir(

   113→            result = train_and_register(

   126→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   127→# Helpers: inject/remove fake GPU modules into sys.modules so deferred imports
   128→# inside train_qlora resolve without requiring real GPU packages.
   129→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   130→
   131→_FAKE_GPU_MODULES = [
   132→    "datasets",
   133→    "torch",
   134→    "transformers",
   135→    "transformers.AutoModelForCausalLM",
   136→    "transformers.AutoTokenizer",
   137→    "transformers.BitsAndBytesConfig",
   138→    "trl",
   139→    "trl.SFTConfig",
   140→    "trl.SFTTrainer",
   141→    "peft",
   142→]
   143→
   144→
   145→def _inject_fake_gpu_modules() -> None:
   146→    """Inject minimal fake modules into sys.modules for CPU-only test runs."""
   147→    fake_torch = ModuleType("torch")
   148→    fake_torch.bfloat16 = MagicMock()  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
   149→
   150→    fake_datasets = ModuleType("datasets")
   151→
   152→    def _fake_from_list(records: list) -> MagicMock:  # type: ignore[type-arg]
   153→        return MagicMock()
   154→
   155→    fake_dataset_cls = MagicMock()
   156→    fake_dataset_cls.from_list = staticmethod(_fake_from_list)
   157→    fake_datasets.Dataset = fake_dataset_cls  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
   158→
   159→    fake_transformers = ModuleType("transformers")
   160→    fake_transformers.AutoModelForCausalLM = MagicMock()  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
   161→    fake_transformers.AutoTokenizer = MagicMock()  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
   162→    fake_transformers.BitsAndBytesConfig = MagicMock()  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
   163→
   164→    fake_trl = ModuleType("trl")
   165→    fake_trl.SFTConfig = MagicMock()  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
   166→    fake_trl.SFTTrainer = MagicMock()  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
   167→
   168→    fake_peft = ModuleType("peft")
   169→    fake_peft.LoraConfig = MagicMock()  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
   170→    fake_peft.get_peft_model = MagicMock()  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
   171→
   172→    sys.modules.setdefault("torch", fake_torch)
   173→    sys.modules.setdefault("datasets", fake_datasets)
   174→    sys.modules.setdefault("transformers", fake_transformers)
   175→    sys.modules.setdefault("trl", fake_trl)
   176→    sys.modules.setdefault("peft", fake_peft)
   177→
   178→
   179→def _remove_fake_gpu_modules() -> None:
   180→    """Remove injected fake modules (only if we injected them)."""
   181→    for mod in ("torch", "datasets", "transformers", "trl", "peft"):
   182→        if mod in sys.modules and isinstance(sys.modules[mod], ModuleType):
   183→            # Only remove if it's actually a fake (not the real package)
   184→            m = sys.modules[mod]
   185→            if getattr(m, "__file__", "FAKE") is None:
   186→                del sys.modules[mod]
   187→

1→"""Training pool management and memory watchdog for swarm execution.
     2→
     3→Provides async workers for managing concurrent training jobs with GPU
     4→time-sharing (sleep/wake for single-GPU mode) and memory pressure monitoring.
     5→"""
     6→
     7→from __future__ import annotations
     8→
     9→import asyncio
    10→import logging
    11→from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor
    12→from dataclasses import dataclass
    13→from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, Any
    14→
    15→if TYPE_CHECKING:
    16→    from shared.hardware import HardwareBudget
    17→
    18→logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
    19→
    20→
    21→@dataclass
    22→class TrainingRequest:
    23→    """A request to train a LoRA adapter.
    24→
    25→    Attributes:
    26→        session_id: Unique session identifier.
    27→        task_type: Task category for training.
    28→    """
    29→
    30→    session_id: str
    31→    task_type: str
    32→
    33→
    34→async def _drain_queue(
    35→    queue: asyncio.Queue[TrainingRequest],
    36→    max_batch: int,
    37→    timeout: float = 1.0,
    38→) -> list[TrainingRequest]:
    39→    """Drain up to max_batch items from an async queue.
    40→
    41→    Args:
    42→        queue: Async queue to drain.
    43→        max_batch: Maximum items to collect.
    44→        timeout: Seconds to wait for the first item.
    45→
    46→    Returns:
    47→        List of collected TrainingRequest items.
    48→    """
    49→    batch: list[TrainingRequest] = []
    50→    try:
    51→        first = await asyncio.wait_for(queue.get(), timeout=timeout)
    52→        batch.append(first)
    53→    except (asyncio.TimeoutError, TimeoutError):
    54→        return batch
    55→
    56→    while len(batch) < max_batch:
    57→        try:
    58→            item = queue.get_nowait()
    59→            batch.append(item)
    60→        except asyncio.QueueEmpty:
    61→            break
    62→    return batch
    63→
    64→
    65→def _train_in_subprocess(
    66→    session_id: str,
    67→    task_type: str,
    68→    adapter_dir: str,
    69→    db_url: str,
    70→) -> dict[str, Any]:
    71→    """Top-level picklable function for training in a subprocess.
    72→
    73→    Imports GPU modules inside function body (INFRA-05 pattern).
    74→
    75→    Args:
    76→        session_id: Unique session identifier.
    77→        task_type: Task category.
    78→        adapter_dir: Directory to save adapter weights.
    79→        db_url: Database URL for the adapter registry.
    80→
    81→    Returns:
    82→        Dict with adapter_id, session_id, task_type, adapter_dir.
    83→    """
    84→    import uuid
    85→
    86→    adapter_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    87→
    88→    try:
    89→        from model_training.trainer import train_qlora
    90→
    91→        train_qlora(
    92→            session_id=session_id,
    93→            task_type=task_type,
    94→            output_dir=adapter_dir,
    95→        )
    96→    except ImportError:
    97→        logger.warning(
    98→            "model_training.trainer not available; skipping actual training "
    99→            "for session %s",
   100→            session_id,
   101→        )
   102→
   103→    return {
   104→        "adapter_id": adapter_id,
   105→        "session_id": session_id,
   106→        "task_type": task_type,
   107→        "adapter_dir": adapter_dir,
   108→    }
   109→
   110→
   111→async def _sleep_vllm(base_url: str) -> None:
   112→    """Tell vLLM to release GPU memory for training.
   113→
   114→    Args:
   115→        base_url: vLLM server base URL.
   116→    """
   117→    try:
   118→        import httpx
   119→
   120→        async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
   121→            await client.post(f"{base_url}/sleep", timeout=30)
   122→    except Exception:
   123→        logger.warning("Failed to sleep vLLM at %s", base_url)
   124→
   125→
   126→async def _wake_vllm(base_url: str) -> None:
   127→    """Tell vLLM to reclaim GPU memory after training.
   128→
   129→    Args:
   130→        base_url: vLLM server base URL.
   131→    """
   132→    try:
   133→        import httpx
   134→
   135→        async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
   136→            await client.post(f"{base_url}/wake_up", timeout=30)
   137→    except Exception:
   138→        logger.warning("Failed to wake vLLM at %s", base_url)
   139→
   140→
   141→async def training_pool_manager(
   142→    training_queue: asyncio.Queue[TrainingRequest],
   143→    budget: "HardwareBudget",
   144→    db_url: str = "sqlite:///rune_swarm.db",
   145→    adapter_base_dir: str = "/tmp/rune_adapters",
   146→    vllm_base_url: str = "http://localhost:8100/v1",
   147→    shutdown_event: asyncio.Event | None = None,
   148→) -> None:
   149→    """Async manager that processes training requests from a queue.
   150→
   151→    Uses ProcessPoolExecutor for CPU-bound training. In single-GPU mode,
   152→    coordinates with vLLM via sleep/wake to time-share the GPU.
   153→
   154→    Args:
   155→        training_queue: Queue of TrainingRequest items.
   156→        budget: Hardware budget for concurrency decisions.
   157→        db_url: Database URL for the adapter registry.
   158→        adapter_base_dir: Base directory for saving adapter weights.
   159→        vllm_base_url: vLLM server URL for sleep/wake coordination.
   160→        shutdown_event: Event to signal graceful shutdown.
   161→    """
   162→    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
   163→    max_workers = max(1, budget.training_slots)
   164→
   165→    with ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=max_workers) as pool:
   166→        while True:
   167→            if shutdown_event and shutdown_event.is_set():
   168→                break
   169→
   170→            batch = await _drain_queue(
   171→                training_queue, max_batch=max_workers, timeout=2.0
   172→            )
   173→            if not batch:
   174→                continue
   175→
   176→            # Single-GPU: sleep vLLM before training
   177→            if budget.single_gpu_mode and budget.training_slots > 0:
   178→                await _sleep_vllm(vllm_base_url)
   179→
   180→            futures = []
   181→            for req in batch:
   182→                adapter_dir = f"{adapter_base_dir}/{req.session_id}"
   183→                future = loop.run_in_executor(
   184→                    pool,
   185→                    _train_in_subprocess,
   186→                    req.session_id,
   187→                    req.task_type,
   188→                    adapter_dir,
   189→                    db_url,
   190→                )
   191→                futures.append(future)
   192→
   193→            results = await asyncio.gather(*futures, return_exceptions=True)
   194→
   195→            # Single-GPU: wake vLLM after training
   196→            if budget.single_gpu_mode and budget.training_slots > 0:
   197→                await _wake_vllm(vllm_base_url)
   198→
   199→            for result in results:
   200→                if isinstance(result, Exception):
   201→                    logger.error("Training failed: %s", result)
   202→                else:
   203→                    logger.info("Training completed: %s", result)
   204→
   205→
   206→async def memory_watchdog(
   207→    budget: "HardwareBudget",
   208→    agents: list[asyncio.Task[Any]],
   209→    check_interval: float = 30.0,
   210→    threshold: float = 0.85,
   211→    shutdown_event: asyncio.Event | None = None,
   212→) -> None:
   213→    """Monitor memory pressure and cancel youngest agent if too high.
   214→
   215→    Args:
   216→        budget: Hardware budget (unused but kept for future VRAM checks).
   217→        agents: Mutable list of running agent tasks.
   218→        check_interval: Seconds between memory checks.
   219→        threshold: Memory usage fraction triggering cancellation.
   220→        shutdown_event: Event to signal graceful shutdown.
   221→    """
   222→    import psutil
   223→
   224→    while True:
   225→        if shutdown_event and shutdown_event.is_set():
   226→            break
   227→
   228→        mem = psutil.virtual_memory()
   229→        usage = mem.percent / 100.0
   230→
   231→        if usage > threshold and agents:
   232→            youngest = agents[-1]
   233→            logger.warning(
   234→                "Memory pressure %.1f%% > %.1f%% threshold, cancelling youngest agent",
   235→                usage * 100,
   236→                threshold * 100,
   237→            )
   238→            youngest.cancel()
   239→            agents.remove(youngest)
   240→
   241→        await asyncio.sleep(check_interval)
   242→

1→"""Evolution worker and sweep logic for swarm adapter evolution.
     2→
     3→Provides async workers that periodically evaluate adapters, merge
     4→top performers via TIES-Merging, and prune low-fitness adapters.
     5→"""
     6→
     7→from __future__ import annotations
     8→
     9→import asyncio
    10→import logging
    11→import uuid
    12→from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, Any
    13→
    14→if TYPE_CHECKING:
    15→    from adapter_registry import AdapterRegistry
    16→
    17→logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
    18→
    19→# Thresholds
    20→MERGE_MIN_ADAPTERS = 5
    21→MERGE_TOP_K = 3
    22→PRUNE_FITNESS_THRESHOLD = 0.3
    23→
    24→
    25→def evaluate_adapter(
    26→    adapter_id: str,
    27→    pass_rate: float,
    28→    diversity_score: float,
    29→    registry: "AdapterRegistry",
    30→) -> float:
    31→    """Evaluate an adapter and update its fitness in the registry.
    32→
    33→    Args:
    34→        adapter_id: ID of the adapter to evaluate.
    35→        pass_rate: Measured pass rate.
    36→        diversity_score: Measured diversity score.
    37→        registry: Adapter registry for persisting results.
    38→
    39→    Returns:
    40→        Computed fitness score.
    41→    """
    42→    from evaluation.metrics import evaluate_fitness
    43→
    44→    fitness = evaluate_fitness(adapter_id, pass_rate, diversity_score)
    45→    registry.update_fitness(adapter_id, pass_rate=pass_rate, fitness_score=fitness)
    46→    return fitness
    47→
    48→
    49→def evolution_sweep(registry: "AdapterRegistry") -> dict[str, Any]:
    50→    """Run one evolution sweep across all task types.
    51→
    52→    For each task type with >= MERGE_MIN_ADAPTERS, TIES-merges the top-3
    53→    adapters. Archives any adapter with fitness < PRUNE_FITNESS_THRESHOLD.
    54→
    55→    Args:
    56→        registry: Adapter registry to query and modify.
    57→
    58→    Returns:
    59→        Summary dict with merged and pruned counts per task type.
    60→    """
    61→    summary: dict[str, Any] = {"task_types": {}}
    62→
    63→    for task_type in registry.get_task_types():
    64→        all_adapters = registry.query_by_task_type(task_type)
    65→        active = [a for a in all_adapters if not a.is_archived]
    66→
    67→        merged_count = 0
    68→        pruned_count = 0
    69→
    70→        # Merge top adapters if we have enough
    71→        if len(active) >= MERGE_MIN_ADAPTERS:
    72→            top = registry.query_best_for_task(task_type, top_k=MERGE_TOP_K)
    73→            if len(top) >= 2:
    74→                parent_ids = [a.id for a in top]
    75→                try:
    76→                    _ties_merge_adapters(parent_ids, task_type, registry)
    77→                    merged_count = 1
    78→                except Exception:
    79→                    logger.exception("TIES merge failed for task_type=%s", task_type)
    80→
    81→        # Prune low-fitness adapters
    82→        for adapter in active:
    83→            if (
    84→                adapter.fitness_score is not None
    85→                and adapter.fitness_score < PRUNE_FITNESS_THRESHOLD
    86→            ):
    87→                registry.archive(adapter.id)
    88→                pruned_count += 1
    89→
    90→        summary["task_types"][task_type] = {
    91→            "merged": merged_count,
    92→            "pruned": pruned_count,
    93→            "active": len(active) - pruned_count + merged_count,
    94→        }
    95→
    96→    return summary
    97→
    98→
    99→def _ties_merge_adapters(
   100→    adapter_ids: list[str],
   101→    task_type: str,
   102→    registry: "AdapterRegistry",
   103→) -> str:
   104→    """Load adapter state dicts, TIES-merge them, and register the result.
   105→
   106→    All GPU imports are deferred (INFRA-05 pattern).
   107→
   108→    Args:
   109→        adapter_ids: IDs of adapters to merge.
   110→        task_type: Task type for the merged adapter.
   111→        registry: Registry for loading adapter paths and storing result.
   112→
   113→    Returns:
   114→        ID of the newly registered merged adapter.
   115→    """
   116→    import json
   117→
   118→    from model_training.merging import load_adapter_state_dict, ties_merge
   119→
   120→    adapters = [registry.retrieve_by_id(aid) for aid in adapter_ids]
   121→    state_dicts = [load_adapter_state_dict(a.file_path) for a in adapters]
   122→    merged_sd = ties_merge(state_dicts)
   123→
   124→    # Save merged adapter
   125→    merged_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
   126→    output_dir = f"/tmp/rune_merged/{merged_id}"
   127→
   128→    import os
   129→
   130→    os.makedirs(output_dir, exist_ok=True)
   131→
   132→    from safetensors.torch import save_file
   133→
   134→    save_file(merged_sd, f"{output_dir}/adapter_model.safetensors")
   135→
   136→    # Register merged adapter
   137→    from adapter_registry.models import AdapterRecord
   138→
   139→    max_gen = max(a.generation for a in adapters)
   140→    record = AdapterRecord(
   141→        id=merged_id,
   142→        version=1,
   143→        task_type=task_type,
   144→        base_model_id=adapters[0].base_model_id,
   145→        rank=adapters[0].rank,
   146→        created_at="",
   147→        file_path=f"{output_dir}/adapter_model.safetensors",
   148→        file_hash="",
   149→        file_size_bytes=0,
   150→        source="evolution",
   151→        session_id="merge",
   152→        parent_ids=json.dumps(adapter_ids),
   153→        generation=max_gen + 1,
   154→    )
   155→    registry.store(record)
   156→    return merged_id
   157→
   158→
   159→async def evolution_worker(
   160→    registry: "AdapterRegistry",
   161→    interval_seconds: int = 7200,
   162→    shutdown_event: asyncio.Event | None = None,
   163→) -> None:
   164→    """Async worker that runs periodic evolution sweeps.
   165→
   166→    Args:
   167→        registry: Adapter registry to operate on.
   168→        interval_seconds: Seconds between sweep runs.
   169→        shutdown_event: Event to signal graceful shutdown.
   170→    """
   171→    while True:
   172→        if shutdown_event and shutdown_event.is_set():
   173→            break
   174→
   175→        try:
   176→            summary = evolution_sweep(registry)
   177→            logger.info("Evolution sweep completed: %s", summary)
   178→        except Exception:
   179→            logger.exception("Evolution sweep failed")
   180→
   181→        await asyncio.sleep(interval_seconds)
   182→

1→"""Adapter merging strategies for evolutionary combination.
     2→
     3→Implements TIES-Merging and DARE-Merging for combining multiple LoRA
     4→adapter state dicts into a single merged adapter. All GPU imports are
     5→deferred inside function bodies (INFRA-05 pattern).
     6→"""
     7→
     8→from __future__ import annotations
     9→
    10→from pathlib import Path
    11→from typing import Any
    12→
    13→
    14→def ties_merge(
    15→    state_dicts: list[dict[str, Any]],
    16→    density: float = 0.5,
    17→) -> dict[str, Any]:
    18→    """Merge adapter state dicts using TIES-Merging.
    19→
    20→    Trim-Elect-Sign-Disjoint merge: for each parameter, trims values
    21→    below density threshold, elects the majority sign, then averages
    22→    only the values matching the elected sign.
    23→
    24→    Args:
    25→        state_dicts: List of state dicts (tensors) to merge.
    26→        density: Fraction of values to keep per parameter (0.0 to 1.0).
    27→
    28→    Returns:
    29→        Merged state dict with same keys and shapes as inputs.
    30→    """
    31→    if not state_dicts:
    32→        return {}
    33→
    34→    import torch
    35→
    36→    merged: dict[str, Any] = {}
    37→    keys = state_dicts[0].keys()
    38→
    39→    for key in keys:
    40→        tensors = [sd[key].float() for sd in state_dicts]
    41→        stacked = torch.stack(tensors)
    42→
    43→        # Trim: zero out values below density threshold per tensor
    44→        for i in range(len(tensors)):
    45→            flat = stacked[i].abs().flatten()
    46→            if flat.numel() == 0:
    47→                continue
    48→            k_keep = max(1, int(flat.numel() * density))
    49→            threshold = torch.topk(flat, k_keep).values[-1]
    50→            mask = stacked[i].abs() >= threshold
    51→            stacked[i] = stacked[i] * mask.float()
    52→
    53→        # Elect sign: majority vote across state dicts
    54→        sign_sum = torch.sign(stacked).sum(dim=0)
    55→        elected_sign = torch.sign(sign_sum)
    56→
    57→        # Disjoint merge: average only values matching elected sign
    58→        matching = (torch.sign(stacked) == elected_sign.unsqueeze(0)).float()
    59→        matching_vals = stacked * matching
    60→        count = matching.sum(dim=0).clamp(min=1)
    61→        merged[key] = (matching_vals.sum(dim=0) / count).to(tensors[0].dtype)
    62→
    63→    return merged
    64→
    65→
    66→def dare_merge(
    67→    state_dicts: list[dict[str, Any]],
    68→    drop_rate: float = 0.1,
    69→) -> dict[str, Any]:
    70→    """Merge adapter state dicts using DARE-Merging.
    71→
    72→    Drop-And-REscale merge: randomly drops a fraction of values from
    73→    each state dict, then averages the remaining values with rescaling.
    74→
    75→    Args:
    76→        state_dicts: List of state dicts to merge.
    77→        drop_rate: Fraction of values to drop per parameter (0.0 to 1.0).
    78→
    79→    Returns:
    80→        Merged state dict with same keys and shapes as inputs.
    81→    """
    82→    if not state_dicts:
    83→        return {}
    84→
    85→    import torch
    86→
    87→    merged: dict[str, Any] = {}
    88→    keys = state_dicts[0].keys()
    89→    scale = 1.0 / (1.0 - drop_rate) if drop_rate < 1.0 else 1.0
    90→
    91→    for key in keys:
    92→        tensors = [sd[key].float() for sd in state_dicts]
    93→        stacked = torch.stack(tensors)
    94→
    95→        # Drop and rescale each tensor
    96→        for i in range(len(tensors)):
    97→            if drop_rate > 0:
    98→                mask = (torch.rand_like(stacked[i]) >= drop_rate).float()
    99→                stacked[i] = stacked[i] * mask * scale
   100→
   101→        # Average across state dicts
   102→        merged[key] = stacked.mean(dim=0).to(tensors[0].dtype)
   103→
   104→    return merged
   105→
   106→
   107→def load_adapter_state_dict(adapter_path: str | Path) -> dict[str, Any]:
   108→    """Load a LoRA adapter state dict from a safetensors file.
   109→
   110→    Args:
   111→        adapter_path: Path to the adapter .safetensors file.
   112→
   113→    Returns:
   114→        State dict mapping parameter names to tensors.
   115→    """
   116→    from safetensors.torch import load_file
   117→
   118→    return load_file(str(adapter_path))
   119→

1→"""Evaluation metrics for adapter benchmarking and fitness scoring.
     2→
     3→Provides functions for running benchmarks (HumanEval), calculating metrics
     4→(Pass@k, quality scores), comparing adapters, testing generalization,
     5→and computing evolutionary fitness for the evolution operator.
     6→"""
     7→
     8→from __future__ import annotations
     9→
    10→import json
    11→import math
    12→import subprocess
    13→import sys
    14→import tempfile
    15→from pathlib import Path
    16→from typing import Any, Optional
    17→
    18→_DATA_DIR = Path(__file__).parent / "data"
    19→
    20→
    21→def calculate_pass_at_k(n_samples: int, n_correct: int, k: int = 1) -> float:
    22→    """Calculate the Pass@k metric for code generation evaluation.
    23→
    24→    Computes the probability that at least one of k sampled solutions is
    25→    correct, using the unbiased estimator from the HumanEval paper
    26→    (Chen et al., 2021). This avoids the high-variance naive estimator.
    27→
    28→    Formula: 1 - prod((n-c-i)/(n-i) for i in range(k))
    29→
    30→    Args:
    31→        n_samples: Total number of samples generated per problem.
    32→        n_correct: Number of correct samples out of n_samples.
    33→        k: Number of attempts allowed for the Pass@k metric. Defaults to 1.
    34→
    35→    Returns:
    36→        Pass@k probability as a float between 0.0 and 1.0.
    37→
    38→    Raises:
    39→        ValueError: If n_correct > n_samples.
    40→
    41→    Example:
    42→        >>> score = calculate_pass_at_k(n_samples=100, n_correct=85, k=1)
    43→        >>> score
    44→        0.85
    45→    """
    46→    if n_correct > n_samples:
    47→        raise ValueError(
    48→            f"n_correct ({n_correct}) cannot be greater than n_samples ({n_samples})"
    49→        )
    50→    if n_correct == n_samples:
    51→        return 1.0
    52→    if n_samples - n_correct < k:
    53→        return 1.0
    54→    # Unbiased estimator: 1 - prod((n-c-i)/(n-i) for i in range(k))
    55→    product = math.prod((n_samples - n_correct - i) / (n_samples - i) for i in range(k))
    56→    return 1.0 - product
    57→
    58→
    59→def run_kill_switch_gate(
    60→    baseline_pass1: float,
    61→    adapter_pass1: float,
    62→    threshold: float = 0.05,
    63→) -> dict[str, object]:
    64→    """Compare baseline vs adapter Pass@1 scores and return a PASS/FAIL verdict.
    65→
    66→    Computes the relative improvement of the adapter over the baseline and
    67→    returns PASS if the improvement meets the threshold, FAIL otherwise.
    68→
    69→    Args:
    70→        baseline_pass1: Pass@1 score for the baseline model (no adapter).
    71→        adapter_pass1: Pass@1 score for the adapter model.
    72→        threshold: Minimum required relative improvement (default 0.05 = 5%).
    73→
    74→    Returns:
    75→        Dictionary with:
    76→            - "baseline_pass1": float
    77→            - "adapter_pass1": float
    78→            - "relative_delta": float, relative improvement over baseline
    79→            - "verdict": str, "PASS" or "FAIL"
    80→
    81→    Example:
    82→        >>> result = run_kill_switch_gate(0.50, 0.55)
    83→        >>> result["verdict"]
    84→        'PASS'
    85→    """
    86→    # 1e-9 prevents division-by-zero when baseline is 0.0
    87→    relative_delta = (adapter_pass1 - baseline_pass1) / max(baseline_pass1, 1e-9)
    88→    verdict = "PASS" if adapter_pass1 >= baseline_pass1 * (1 + threshold) else "FAIL"
    89→
    90→    print(
    91→        f"Kill-switch gate result: {verdict}\n"
    92→        f"  Baseline  Pass@1: {baseline_pass1:.4f}\n"
    93→        f"  Adapter   Pass@1: {adapter_pass1:.4f}\n"
    94→        f"  Relative delta:   {relative_delta:+.2%} (threshold: {threshold:+.0%})"
    95→    )
    96→
    97→    return {
    98→        "baseline_pass1": baseline_pass1,
    99→        "adapter_pass1": adapter_pass1,
   100→        "relative_delta": relative_delta,
   101→        "verdict": verdict,
   102→    }
   103→
   104→
   105→def run_humaneval_subset(
   106→    adapter_id: Optional[str],
   107→    subset_size: int = 20,
   108→    model: str = "Qwen/Qwen2.5-Coder-7B",
   109→    completions: Optional[dict[str, str]] = None,
   110→) -> dict[str, Any]:
   111→    """Run a HumanEval benchmark subset to evaluate an adapter.
   112→
   113→    Loads tasks from the bundled 20-task HumanEval subset JSON and evaluates
   114→    them using the provided completions. For each task, concatenates
   115→    prompt + completion + test + check(entry_point), writes to a temp script,
   116→    and executes via subprocess. Exit code 0 = passed.
   117→
   118→    Args:
   119→        adapter_id: UUID of the adapter to test (None = baseline, no adapter).
   120→            Currently informational; inference wiring happens at a higher level.
   121→        subset_size: Ignored — always uses the fixed 20-task bundled subset.
   122→        model: Base model name (informational only, not used for inference here).
   123→        completions: Dict mapping task_id -> completion string. Only tasks with
   124→            an entry in this dict are evaluated. If None or empty, returns empty
   125→            results.
   126→
   127→    Returns:
   128→        Dictionary with benchmark results including:
   129→            - "pass_count": int, number of tasks passed
   130→            - "fail_count": int, number of tasks failed
   131→            - "pass_rate": float, fraction of tasks passed (0.0 to 1.0)
   132→            - "task_results": list of per-task result dicts with task_id, passed
   133→            - "summary": str, human-readable summary
   134→
   135→    Raises:
   136→        NotImplementedError: If completions are not provided (inference not wired).
   137→
   138→    Example:
   139→        >>> completions = {"HumanEval/0": "    return []"}
   140→        >>> results = run_humaneval_subset(adapter_id=None, completions=completions)
   141→        >>> results["pass_count"]
   142→        0
   143→    """
   144→    if completions is None:
   145→        raise NotImplementedError("run_humaneval_subset is not yet implemented.")
   146→
   147→    # Load bundled task data
   148→    subset_path = _DATA_DIR / "humaneval_subset.json"
   149→    with subset_path.open() as f:
   150→        all_tasks: list[dict[str, str]] = json.load(f)
   151→
   152→    # Build lookup by task_id
   153→    task_map = {t["task_id"]: t for t in all_tasks}
   154→
   155→    task_results: list[dict[str, Any]] = []
   156→
   157→    with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as tmpdir:
   158→        for task_id, completion in completions.items():
   159→            task = task_map.get(task_id)
   160→            if task is None:
   161→                continue
   162→
   163→            # Build executable script: prompt + completion + test + check(entry_point)
   164→            script = (
   165→                task["prompt"]
   166→                + completion
   167→                + "\n"
   168→                + task["test"]
   169→                + f"\ncheck({task['entry_point']})\n"
   170→            )
   171→
   172→            script_path = Path(tmpdir) / f"{task_id.replace('/', '_')}.py"
   173→            script_path.write_text(script)
   174→
   175→            proc = subprocess.run(
   176→                [sys.executable, str(script_path)],
   177→                capture_output=True,
   178→                text=True,
   179→                timeout=30,
   180→                cwd=tmpdir,
   181→            )
   182→            passed = proc.returncode == 0
   183→            task_results.append({"task_id": task_id, "passed": passed})
   184→
   185→    pass_count = sum(1 for r in task_results if r["passed"])
   186→    fail_count = len(task_results) - pass_count
   187→    total = len(task_results)
   188→    pass_rate = pass_count / total if total > 0 else 0.0
   189→
   190→    summary = (
   191→        f"HumanEval subset: {pass_count}/{total} passed "
   192→        f"(pass_rate={pass_rate:.2%}, adapter_id={adapter_id})"
   193→    )
   194→    print(summary)
   195→
   196→    return {
   197→        "pass_count": pass_count,
   198→        "fail_count": fail_count,
   199→        "pass_rate": pass_rate,
   200→        "task_results": task_results,
   201→        "summary": summary,
   202→    }
   203→
   204→
   205→def score_adapter_quality(
   206→    adapter_id: str,
   207→    pass_rate: float,
   208→    generalization_delta: float | None = None,
   209→) -> float:
   210→    """Compute an overall quality score for an adapter.
   211→
   212→    Aggregates the adapter's benchmark pass rate with its generalization
   213→    performance (if available) into a single scalar quality score. When
   214→    generalization data is absent, quality is derived from pass rate alone.
   215→
   216→    Args:
   217→        adapter_id: UUID of the adapter to score.
   218→        pass_rate: Fraction of benchmark tasks passed, in range 0.0 to 1.0.
   219→        generalization_delta: Optional difference between in-distribution and
   220→            out-of-distribution (OOD) performance. Positive values indicate
   221→            the adapter generalizes well; negative values indicate overfitting.
   222→            Defaults to None (not measured).
   223→
   224→    Returns:
   225→        Quality score as a float between 0.0 and 1.0. Higher values indicate
   226→        a better overall adapter quality.
   227→
   228→    Raises:
   229→        NotImplementedError: score_adapter_quality is not yet implemented.
   230→
   231→    Example:
   232→        >>> score = score_adapter_quality("adapter-001", pass_rate=0.85)
   233→        >>> score
   234→        0.85
   235→        >>> score_with_gen = score_adapter_quality(
   236→        ...     "adapter-001", 0.85, generalization_delta=0.1,
   237→        ... )
   238→        >>> score_with_gen > score
   239→        True
   240→    """
   241→    if generalization_delta is not None:
   242→        quality = min(pass_rate + 0.1 * max(generalization_delta, 0), 1.0)
   243→    else:
   244→        quality = pass_rate
   245→    return quality
   246→
   247→
   248→def compare_adapters(
   249→    adapter_ids: list[str],
   250→    benchmark: str = "humaneval",
   251→) -> dict[str, Any]:
   252→    """Compare multiple adapters head-to-head on a benchmark.
   253→
   254→    Evaluates each adapter in the list on the specified benchmark and produces
   255→    a comparative report with per-adapter scores, rankings, and a summary of
   256→    which adapter performs best.
   257→
   258→    Args:
   259→        adapter_ids: List of adapter UUIDs to compare. Must contain at least
   260→            two adapter IDs.
   261→        benchmark: Benchmark name to use for comparison. Currently supports
   262→            "humaneval". Defaults to "humaneval".
   263→
   264→    Returns:
   265→        Dictionary with comparison results including:
   266→            - "scores": dict mapping adapter_id to its benchmark score
   267→            - "rankings": list of adapter_ids sorted best-to-worst
   268→            - "best_adapter": str, UUID of the top-performing adapter
   269→            - "summary": str, human-readable comparison summary
   270→
   271→    Raises:
   272→        NotImplementedError: compare_adapters is not yet implemented.
   273→
   274→    Example:
   275→        >>> results = compare_adapters(["adapter-001", "adapter-002"])
   276→        >>> results["best_adapter"] in ["adapter-001", "adapter-002"]
   277→        True
   278→        >>> results["best_adapter"] in results["rankings"]
   279→        True
   280→    """
   281→    if len(adapter_ids) < 2:
   282→        raise ValueError("compare_adapters requires at least 2 adapter IDs")
   283→
   284→    # Without live inference, return a stub comparison based on adapter order
   285→    scores: dict[str, float] = {}
   286→    for i, aid in enumerate(adapter_ids):
   287→        scores[aid] = 1.0 / (i + 1)  # Placeholder scoring
   288→
   289→    rankings = sorted(scores, key=lambda x: scores[x], reverse=True)
   290→    best = rankings[0]
   291→    summary = f"Compared {len(adapter_ids)} adapters on {benchmark}; best={best}"
   292→
   293→    return {
   294→        "scores": scores,
   295→        "rankings": rankings,
   296→        "best_adapter": best,
   297→        "summary": summary,
   298→    }
   299→
   300→
   301→def test_generalization(
   302→    adapter_id: str,
   303→    in_distribution_tasks: list[str] | None = None,
   304→    ood_tasks: list[str] | None = None,
   305→) -> dict[str, Any]:
   306→    """Test whether an adapter generalizes beyond its training distribution.
   307→
   308→    Evaluates the adapter on both in-distribution tasks (matching the training
   309→    data distribution) and out-of-distribution (OOD) tasks to measure how well
   310→    the adapter generalizes to novel problems.
   311→
   312→    Args:
   313→        adapter_id: UUID of the adapter to evaluate.
   314→        in_distribution_tasks: Optional list of task IDs matching the adapter's
   315→            training distribution. If None, uses a default in-distribution set.
   316→        ood_tasks: Optional list of out-of-distribution task IDs to test
   317→            generalization on. If None, uses a default OOD task set.
   318→
   319→    Returns:
   320→        Dictionary with generalization results including:
   321→            - "in_distribution_score": float, performance on training-distribution tasks
   322→            - "ood_score": float, performance on out-of-distribution tasks
   323→            - "generalization_delta": float, difference (in_distribution - ood)
   324→            - "generalizes": bool, True if generalization_delta is within threshold
   325→
   326→    Raises:
   327→        NotImplementedError: test_generalization is not yet implemented.
   328→
   329→    Example:
   330→        >>> results = test_generalization("adapter-001")
   331→        >>> results["generalization_delta"]
   332→        0.05
   333→        >>> results["generalizes"]
   334→        True
   335→    """
   336→    in_dist_score = 0.8  # Placeholder until live inference wired
   337→    ood_score = 0.6
   338→    gen_delta = in_dist_score - ood_score
   339→    generalizes = abs(gen_delta) <= 0.2
   340→
   341→    return {
   342→        "adapter_id": adapter_id,
   343→        "in_distribution_score": in_dist_score,
   344→        "ood_score": ood_score,
   345→        "generalization_delta": gen_delta,
   346→        "generalizes": generalizes,
   347→    }
   348→
   349→
   350→def evaluate_fitness(
   351→    adapter_id: str,
   352→    pass_rate: float,
   353→    diversity_score: float = 0.0,
   354→) -> float:
   355→    """Calculate evolutionary fitness score for the evolution operator.
   356→
   357→    Computes a composite fitness score used by the evolutionary algorithm to
   358→    rank and select adapters for mutation and crossover. Balances raw
   359→    performance (pass_rate) with adapter diversity to avoid population collapse.
   360→
   361→    Args:
   362→        adapter_id: UUID of the adapter to evaluate.
   363→        pass_rate: Fraction of benchmark tasks passed, in range 0.0 to 1.0.
   364→        diversity_score: Adapter uniqueness metric representing how different
   365→            this adapter is from others in the current population. Range 0.0
   366→            to 1.0; higher values indicate more unique adapters. Defaults to 0.0.
   367→
   368→    Returns:
   369→        Fitness score as a float between 0.0 and 1.0. Higher values indicate
   370→        adapters more likely to be selected for the next evolutionary generation.
   371→
   372→    Raises:
   373→        NotImplementedError: evaluate_fitness is not yet implemented.
   374→
   375→    Example:
   376→        >>> fitness = evaluate_fitness(
   377→        ...     "adapter-001", pass_rate=0.85, diversity_score=0.3,
   378→        ... )
   379→        >>> fitness
   380→        0.795
   381→        >>> low_diversity = evaluate_fitness(
   382→        ...     "adapter-001", pass_rate=0.85, diversity_score=0.0,
   383→        ... )
   384→        >>> low_diversity < fitness
   385→        True
   386→    """
   387→    fitness = 0.7 * pass_rate + 0.3 * diversity_score
   388→    return fitness
   389→

1→"""QLoRA PEFT configuration and adapter management.
     2→
     3→All GPU library imports (peft, transformers, torch) are deferred inside
     4→function bodies to ensure CPU-only importability (INFRA-05).
     5→"""
     6→
     7→from __future__ import annotations
     8→
     9→from typing import Any
    10→
    11→
    12→def build_qlora_config(
    13→    rank: int,
    14→    alpha: int,
    15→    target_modules: list[str],
    16→    dropout: float = 0.1,
    17→) -> Any:
    18→    """Build a QLoRA configuration for PEFT fine-tuning.
    19→
    20→    Args:
    21→        rank: LoRA rank (dimensionality of low-rank matrices).
    22→        alpha: LoRA alpha scaling factor.
    23→        target_modules: List of module names to apply LoRA to.
    24→        dropout: Dropout probability for LoRA layers.
    25→
    26→    Returns:
    27→        A peft LoraConfig instance configured for QLoRA.
    28→
    29→    Example:
    30→        >>> config = build_qlora_config(rank=64, alpha=128, target_modules=["q_proj"])
    31→    """
    32→    from peft import LoraConfig  # deferred — GPU/peft not available in CPU CI
    33→
    34→    return LoraConfig(
    35→        r=rank,
    36→        lora_alpha=alpha,
    37→        target_modules=target_modules,
    38→        lora_dropout=dropout,
    39→        bias="none",
    40→        task_type="CAUSAL_LM",
    41→    )
    42→
    43→
    44→def apply_lora_adapter(model: Any, config: Any) -> Any:
    45→    """Apply a LoRA adapter to a base model.
    46→
    47→    Args:
    48→        model: The base model to wrap with LoRA.
    49→        config: The LoRA configuration (from build_qlora_config).
    50→
    51→    Returns:
    52→        The model wrapped with a LoRA adapter via peft.get_peft_model.
    53→
    54→    Example:
    55→        >>> adapted_model = apply_lora_adapter(base_model, lora_config)
    56→    """
    57→    from peft import get_peft_model  # deferred — GPU/peft not available in CPU CI
    58→
    59→    return get_peft_model(model, config)
    60→
    61→
    62→def merge_adapter(model: Any) -> Any:
    63→    """Merge LoRA weights into the base model.
    64→
    65→    Args:
    66→        model: A PEFT model with LoRA adapter applied.
    67→
    68→    Returns:
    69→        The base model with LoRA weights merged in.
    70→
    71→    Raises:
    72→        NotImplementedError: Adapter merging is out of scope for Phase 21.
    73→
    74→    Example:
    75→        >>> merged = merge_adapter(peft_model)
    76→    """
    77→    raise NotImplementedError(
    78→        "merge_adapter is not yet implemented. "
    79→        "It will merge LoRA weights into the base model and return the merged model."
    80→    )

1→"""Tests for scripts/swarm_evolution.py — evolution sweep and evaluation."""
     2→
     3→import sys
     4→from pathlib import Path
     5→from unittest.mock import patch
     6→
     7→from adapter_registry import AdapterRegistry
     8→from sqlalchemy.pool import StaticPool
     9→from sqlmodel import create_engine
    10→
    11→sys.path.insert(0, str(Path(__file__).parent.parent / "scripts"))
    12→
    13→from swarm_evolution import (
    14→    MERGE_MIN_ADAPTERS,
    15→    PRUNE_FITNESS_THRESHOLD,
    16→    evaluate_adapter,
    17→    evolution_sweep,
    18→)
    19→
    20→
    21→def _make_registry():
    22→    engine = create_engine(
    23→        "sqlite:///:memory:",
    24→        connect_args={"check_same_thread": False},
    25→        poolclass=StaticPool,
    26→    )
    27→    return AdapterRegistry(engine=engine)
    28→
    29→
    30→def _make_record(registry, adapter_id, task_type="bug-fix", fitness=None, **kwargs):
    31→    from adapter_registry.models import AdapterRecord
    32→
    33→    record = AdapterRecord(
    34→        id=adapter_id,
    35→        version=1,
    36→        task_type=task_type,
    37→        base_model_id="Qwen/Qwen2.5-Coder-7B",
    38→        rank=16,
    39→        created_at="2026-01-01T00:00:00Z",
    40→        file_path=f"/adapters/{adapter_id}.safetensors",
    41→        file_hash="abc123",
    42→        file_size_bytes=1024,
    43→        pass_rate=fitness,
    44→        fitness_score=fitness,
    45→        source="distillation",
    46→        session_id="test-session",
    47→        **kwargs,
    48→    )
    49→    registry.store(record)
    50→    return record
    51→
    52→
    53→def test_evaluate_adapter_updates_registry() -> None:
    54→    registry = _make_registry()
    55→    _make_record(registry, "a1")
    56→    fitness = evaluate_adapter(
    57→        "a1", pass_rate=0.8, diversity_score=0.2, registry=registry
    58→    )
    59→    record = registry.retrieve_by_id("a1")
    60→    assert record.pass_rate == 0.8
    61→    assert record.fitness_score == fitness
    62→    assert fitness > 0
    63→
    64→
    65→def test_evolution_sweep_prunes_low_fitness() -> None:
    66→    registry = _make_registry()
    67→    _make_record(registry, "good", fitness=0.8)
    68→    _make_record(registry, "bad", fitness=0.1)
    69→    evolution_sweep(registry)
    70→    bad_record = registry.retrieve_by_id("bad")
    71→    assert bad_record.is_archived is True
    72→    good_record = registry.retrieve_by_id("good")
    73→    assert good_record.is_archived is False
    74→
    75→
    76→def test_evolution_sweep_skips_when_few_adapters() -> None:
    77→    registry = _make_registry()
    78→    for i in range(MERGE_MIN_ADAPTERS - 1):
    79→        _make_record(registry, f"a{i}", fitness=0.8)
    80→    with patch("swarm_evolution._ties_merge_adapters") as mock_merge:
    81→        evolution_sweep(registry)
    82→        mock_merge.assert_not_called()
    83→
    84→
    85→def test_evolution_sweep_merges_when_enough_adapters() -> None:
    86→    registry = _make_registry()
    87→    for i in range(MERGE_MIN_ADAPTERS):
    88→        _make_record(registry, f"a{i}", fitness=0.5 + i * 0.05)

file.content:
# Rune

**Treating LoRA weight space as episodic memory for unbounded context in local coding agents**

---

## Vision

### The Context Window Problem

Current coding agents face a fundamental architectural constraint: as codebases grow, token-based context windows become bloated, expensive, and ultimately insufficient. Even with 400K-token windows, maintaining state across long coding sessions requires quadratic compute overhead and suffers from "needle in a haystack" retrieval failures. For local Small Language Models (SLMs) under 10B parameters, this limitation is existential.

Rune addresses this by **moving state representation from the token-level context window into the model's weight-space** via dynamic, task-specific LoRA adapters. Rather than stuffing ever-more tokens into context, we encode procedural knowledge—architecture specifications, execution feedback, coding conventions—directly into the model's parameters as modular, composable memory units.

### The Solution: Parametric Episodic Memory

Rune implements a **recursive code generation loop** where each iteration:

1. **Generates code** using the current adapter state
2. **Executes** in a sandboxed environment
3. **Distills feedback** (errors, test results, architectural constraints) into LoRA weight updates via a hypernetwork
4. **Composes memory** by merging task-specific adapters into a persistent library

**Initial Bootstrapping**: Before the first code generation iteration, the hypernetwork processes the project specification—goals, implementation rules, architecture conventions, and organizational structure—to create an initial project-specific adapter. This ensures the base model is immediately contextualized to the project's requirements, rather than starting from a generic coding baseline.

This transforms coding from a stateless LLM call into a **sequential decision-making process with parameter-space memory**. The agent doesn't just retrieve documentation—it internalizes procedural logic as trainable weight deltas, creating "skill-based memory" that persists across sessions.

**Core Innovation**: We treat the LoRA weight space as episodic memory. Each coding trajectory becomes a reusable adapter that captures not just what was done, but how to do it. By composing these adapters, the agent builds an unbounded, hierarchical memory structure that scales independently of context window size.

---

## Architecture Overview

### The Recursive Loop

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Base SLM (<10B params) + Active LoRA Adapter(s)       │
└──────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘
                   │
                   ▼
         ┌─────────────────┐
         │  Code Generation │
         └────────┬─────────┘
                  │
                  ▼
         ┌─────────────────┐
         │ Sandbox Execution│
         └────────┬─────────┘
                  │
                  ▼
    ┌────────────────────────────┐
    │  System-2 Reasoning Loop   │
    │  (Error Analysis + Logic   │
    │   Correction + Test Review)│
    └─────────┬──────────────────┘
              │
              ▼
    ┌──────────────────────────┐
    │  Doc-to-LoRA Hypernetwork│
    │  (Feedback → ΔW encoding)│
    └──────────┬───────────────┘
               │
               ▼
    ┌──────────────────────────┐
    │   Evolution Operator (E) │
    │  (Consolidate/Update/    │
    │   Forget/Merge Adapters) │
    └──────────┬───────────────┘
               │
               └──────► Update Active Adapters
                        │
                        └──► Persist to Adapter Library

### Key Components

**1. Doc-to-LoRA Hypernetwork**

A lightweight meta-learning network that maps execution traces to LoRA parameters, inspired by Sakana AI's Doc-to-LoRA architecture[44]:

- **Input**: Project documentation, architecture specs, error traces, test results
- **Output**: Rank-decomposed LoRA matrices (ΔW = BA, where B ∈ ℝ^(d×r), A ∈ ℝ^(r×d))
- **Architecture**: Perceiver-based encoder to handle variable-length inputs, chunk mechanism for contexts exceeding base model window
- **Training**: Meta-trained on diverse coding trajectories to learn the mapping from feedback → parameter updates

**Initial Pass Usage**: Unlike the original Doc-to-LoRA which focuses on knowledge updates, Rune extends this to project initialization. On the first invocation, the hypernetwork processes project specifications (goals, coding standards, architecture patterns, file organization) to generate an initial adapter that contextualizes the base model to the specific project domain before any code is generated.

**2. Evolution Operator**

Manages adapter lifecycle with three operations:

- **Consolidate**: Merge related adapters (e.g., "React hooks" + "useEffect error handling" → "React state management")
- **Update**: Refine existing adapters with new feedback
- **Forget**: Prune low-utility adapters to maintain global coherence
- **Conflict Resolution**: Detects architectural misalignments between adapters using structural graph analysis

**3. Dynamic Adapter Serving**

Built on S-LoRA principles for scalable multi-adapter hosting:

- **Unified Paging**: Shared memory pool for KV cache + adapter weights, reduces fragmentation
- **Lazy Loading**: Adapters loaded from CPU/disk just-in-time as requests arrive
- **Heterogeneous Batching**: Custom CUDA kernels for efficient mixed-rank adapter computation
- **Tiered Weight Caching**: Hot adapters on GPU, warm on CPU, cold on disk

**4. System-2 Reasoning Loop**

Before updating weights, runs structured verification:

- Execute code in isolated sandbox (Docker/Firecracker)
- Run test suite and static analysis
- LLM-based error explanation and logic correction
- Security review (prevents "vibe coding" failures like the Moltbook breach)
- Only successful trajectories distilled into adapters

### Memory Composition Strategy

Adapters are organized hierarchically:

Project Root Adapter
├── Architecture Adapter (framework conventions, design patterns)
├── Domain Adapters
│   ├── Frontend (React, state management, routing)
│   ├── Backend (API design, database schemas)
│   └── Infrastructure (deployment, CI/CD)
└── Task-Specific Adapters
    ├── Bug Fix #123 (specific logic correction)
    ├── Feature: OAuth (implementation trajectory)
    └── Refactor: Extract Service (architectural change)

At inference, the Evolution Operator selects and merges relevant adapters to construct the optimal parameter-space encoding for the current task. This creates a **compositional memory** where complex behaviors emerge from combining simpler, reusable skills.

---

## Core Tech Stack

### Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)

**LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)**[7]

Chosen for high-frequency state updates with minimal interference:

- Parallel injection of rank-decomposition matrices (ΔW = BA)
- Modular: adapters swap without disturbing base model
- Enables "skill-based memory" distillation
- Typical rank: r = 8-64 for coding tasks

**QLoRA (Quantized LoRA)**[27]

4-bit quantization of base model weights:

- Reduces memory from 16 bytes to 7 bytes per parameter (AdamW)
- Enables <10B models on 24GB consumer GPUs
- NF4 quantization maintains full 16-bit finetuning performance
- Critical for local deployment constraints

**QDoRA (Quantized Decomposed LoRA)**[30]

Decomposes weight updates into magnitude and direction:

- More closely mimics full fine-tuning dynamics
- Faster convergence on complex reasoning tasks
- Compatible with 4-bit quantization
- Future enhancement for evolution operator

### Multi-Adapter Serving

**S-LoRA (Scalable LoRA Serving)**[7][12]

Foundation for hosting thousands of adapters:

- Unified Paging: single memory pool for KV cache + adapters
- 4× throughput improvement over naive vLLM
- Serve 2,000+ adapters on single GPU
- Custom CUDA kernels for heterogeneous batching

**LoRAX (LoRA eXchange)**[22][25]

Production-grade serving infrastructure:

- Dynamic adapter loading (just-in-time)
- Continuous multi-adapter batching
- Tiered weight caching (GPU → CPU → disk)
- REST API for adapter selection per request

**LMCache**

KV cache optimization:

- 2.18× lower latency, 2.65× faster TTFT
- Persistent state caching for tool-calling
- 29× speedup for multi-tool agents

### Hypernetwork Architecture

**Perceiver-based Encoder**[44]

Variable-length input handling:

- Maps arbitrary context length to fixed-size latent
- Cross-attention between context and learned queries
- Enables generalization to contexts longer than training sequences

**Chunking Mechanism**[44]

For contexts exceeding base model window:

- Split input into fixed-size chunks (1024 tokens default)
- Generate rank-r LoRA per chunk
- Compose via rank concatenation: effective rank = r × K chunks
- Handles documents up to 128K tokens (trained on 256 tokens)

**Rune-Specific Modifications to Doc-to-LoRA**:

The original Doc-to-LoRA paper[44] demonstrates instant knowledge internalization from documents—enabling a model to answer questions about newly provided information without including that information in the prompt. Rune extends this paradigm in three critical ways:

1. **Project Initialization**: While Doc-to-LoRA focuses on factual knowledge internalization, Rune applies the same principle to procedural knowledge. The first hypernetwork invocation encodes project goals, coding conventions, architectural patterns, and organizational structure into an initial adapter before any code generation begins.

2. **Recursive Refinement**: Doc-to-LoRA is typically one-shot (document → adapter). Rune implements a recursive feedback loop where execution results (errors, test failures, architectural misalignments) are fed back into the hypernetwork to generate incremental adapter updates, creating a trajectory of progressive refinement.

3. **Compositional Memory**: Rather than replacing adapters, Rune maintains a library of task-specific adapters that can be composed. The Evolution Operator merges related adapters (e.g., "React conventions" + "error handling patterns" → "React best practices"), creating hierarchical memory structures that scale beyond single-document encoding.

**Layer-wise Output Heads**

Per-layer LoRA generation:

- Separate heads produce A and B matrices for each target layer
- Typically targets attention (Q, K, V, O) and MLP (up, down) projections
- Enables fine-grained control over which layers adapt

### Optimization Infrastructure

**FlashOptim**

Memory-efficient optimizer states:

- Reduces AdamW memory from 16 → 7 bytes (or 5 with gradient release)
- Gradient checkpointing for activation memory
- Essential for consumer hardware constraints

**Mixed Precision Training**

BF16/FP16 compute with FP32 accumulation:

- Speeds training 2-3× over FP32
- Minimal accuracy loss for most tasks
- Hardware acceleration on modern GPUs

**Run:ai GPU Fractioning**

Multi-tenancy for adapter training:

- Run two independent training jobs on single H100/A100
- 77% throughput per job vs dedicated GPU
- Maximizes hardware utilization

---

## Why These Technologies?

**S-LoRA over alternatives**: PEFT library lacks memory management, vLLM has naive adapter support. S-LoRA's unified paging and custom kernels are purpose-built for this paradigm.

**QLoRA as baseline**: 4-bit quantization is non-negotiable for local deployment. QLoRA's NF4 maintains quality while enabling 65B-class models on 48GB GPUs.

**LoRAX for production**: Dynamic loading and tiered caching match our access patterns. Most adapters are cold (archived trajectories), few are hot (active development).

**Perceiver for hypernetwork**: Context length variability is inherent to coding tasks. Perceiver's cross-attention handles 256-token and 32K-token documents with same architecture.

**Evolution Operator necessity**: Without consolidation, adapter library explodes. Without forgetting, conflicting trajectories degrade performance. The operator maintains global coherence.

---

## Current Status

**⚠️ Active Research - Early Implementation Phase**

This is an **experimental system** exploring parametric episodic memory for local coding agents. The core innovation—treating LoRA weight space as composable memory—is theoretically grounded but requires empirical validation across diverse coding tasks.

**What exists today:**

- Architectural specification (this document)
- Literature review and technical evaluation
- Technology selection and rationale

**What does not exist yet:**

- Working implementation
- Trained hypernetwork
- Adapter library infrastructure
- Empirical performance data

**Open questions:**

- Optimal rank selection per task type
- Adapter composition strategies at scale
- Catastrophic forgetting mitigation
- Security model for sandboxed execution
- Evaluation metrics for parametric memory quality

This repository will document the journey from concept to production-grade system. Contributions, critiques, and empirical insights are welcomed.

---

## Theoretical Grounding

### Comparison: Context-Based vs Parametric Memory

| Dimension | Context-Based State (ICL) | Parametric Episodic Memory |
|-----------|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Capacity Limit** | Capped by token window (e.g., 400K) | Virtually unlimited via adapter libraries |
| **Compute Overhead** | Quadratic scaling with context | Fixed overhead via adapter switching |
| **Latent Knowledge Retention** | Linear; prone to retrieval noise | Stable; high-fidelity weight-space encoding |
| **Persistence** | Volatile; lost after session | Persistent across sessions and tasks |
| **Cost Model** | Pay per token at inference | Pay once during adapter training |

### Why Parameter-Space Encoding?

1. **Capacity**: 10,000× storage reduction via LoRA vs full checkpoints[1]
2. **Speed**: 152,694 tokens/sec throughput with GPU fractioning[1]
3. **Latency**: 29× speedup for multi-tool agents via state caching[1]
4. **Memory**: <50 MB per adapter vs 12+ GB for equivalent context[44]
5. **Quality**: Competitive with frontier models when knowledge is internalized[1]

### Analogy: GSDiff for Code

The Doc-to-LoRA engine draws inspiration from GSDiff (Geometry-enhanced Structural Graph Generation)[1]. Just as GSDiff uses geometry-enhanced optimization to ensure structural integrity in vector floorplans (preventing gaps/overlaps), our hypernetwork ensures **architectural coherence** in codebases by aligning weight updates with the project's structural graph.

This bypasses the "Needle in a Haystack" problem: instead of searching through massive context, relevant state is baked into weights and activated compositionally.

---

## Design Principles

1. **Local-First**: Runs on consumer hardware (24GB VRAM). No API dependencies.
2. **Sovereign AI**: Full control over model, data, and memory. Resilient to vendor lock-in.
3. **Empirically Grounded**: Every architectural choice backed by published research and performance metrics.
4. **Security-Aware**: System-2 reasoning loops prevent "vibe coding" failures. Code review before weight updates.
5. **Compositional Memory**: Complex behaviors emerge from combining simple, reusable adapters.
6. **Transparent**: All adapter updates are auditable. Clear lineage from feedback → weight changes.

---

## References

[1] Technical Evaluation: Parametric Episodic Memory via Hypernetwork-Driven LoRA Adaptation for Local SLMs (Attached Report)

[7] Sheng, Y., et al. (2023). S-LoRA: Serving Thousands of Concurrent LoRA Adapters. arXiv:2311.03285.

[12] Cao, S., et al. (2024). S-LoRA: Scalable Serving of Many LoRA Adapters. MLSys 2024.

[22] Predibase. (2023). LoRAX: LoRA eXchange for Multi-Tenant LLM Serving.

[25] Rubrik. (2023). LoRAX: Open Source Framework for Serving 100s of Fine-Tuned LLMs.

[27] Dettmers, T., et al. (2023). QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs. arXiv:2305.14314.

[30] QDyLoRA: Quantized Dynamic Low-Rank Adaptation for Efficient Large Language Model Fine-tuning. EMNLP 2024.

[41] Ha, D., Dai, A., & Le, Q. V. (2016). HyperNetworks. arXiv:1609.09106.

[44] Sakana AI. (2026). Doc-to-LoRA: Learning to Instantly Internalize Contexts. arXiv:2602.15902. https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.15902

[45] Zheng, Q., et al. (2022). Episodic retrieval for model-based evaluation in sequential decision making. Princeton Neuroscience Institute.

**Note on Doc-to-LoRA Adaptation**: The original Doc-to-LoRA paper demonstrates instant knowledge internalization from text documents. Rune extends this architecture in three ways: (1) project initialization encoding procedural knowledge (goals, conventions, architecture) before first code generation, (2) recursive refinement via feedback loops rather than one-shot encoding, and (3) compositional memory through the Evolution Operator's adapter merging and hierarchical organization, enabling unbounded memory growth beyond single-document limits.

---

# Phased Implementation Plan

## Phase 1: Foundation & Infrastructure

**Objective**: Establish the minimal working infrastructure for adapter training, serving, and sandboxed execution.

### 1.1 Base Model Selection & Quantization

**Tasks**:
- Select base SLM (8-10B params): Llama 3.1 8B or Qwen 2.5 Coder 7B
- Implement 4-bit quantization pipeline using QLoRA
  - NF4 quantization with bitsandbytes
  - Double quantization for constants
  - Paged optimizers for memory spikes
- Benchmark inference throughput and memory footprint
- Establish baseline coding performance on HumanEval, MBPP

**Deliverables**:
- Quantized base model checkpoint
- Inference server with API endpoint
- Performance benchmark report

**Dependencies**: None

**Estimated Time**: 2 weeks

### 1.2 Adapter Serving Infrastructure

**Tasks**:
- Set up S-LoRA/LoRAX serving layer
  - Unified paging implementation
  - Adapter registry (filesystem-based initially)
  - Dynamic loading with LRU eviction policy
- Implement adapter composition at inference time
  - Simple concatenation strategy for MVP
  - Request-level adapter selection API
- Deploy on local hardware (24GB VRAM target)
- Load testing with 10-100 dummy adapters

**Deliverables**:
- Multi-adapter serving endpoint
- Adapter management CLI (load, list, evict)
- Load test results

**Dependencies**: 1.1 (base model)

**Estimated Time**: 3 weeks

### 1.3 Sandboxed Execution Environment

**Tasks**:
- Set up Docker-based code execution sandbox
  - Isolated network namespace
  - Resource limits (CPU, memory, disk)
  - Timeout enforcement (30s default)
- Implement execution harness
  - Capture stdout, stderr, exit codes
  - Parse error traces into structured format
  - Test suite execution and result aggregation
- Security hardening
  - Readonly filesystem except designated output dirs
  - No privilege escalation
  - Syscall filtering (seccomp)

**Deliverables**:
- Execution sandbox Docker image
- Python harness for code submission → results
- Security audit checklist

**Dependencies**: None

**Estimated Time**: 2 weeks

### 1.4 Data Pipeline for Meta-Training

**Tasks**:
- Curate initial training dataset
  - 100-500 coding trajectories (problem → iterations → solution)
  - Include successful and failed attempts
  - Cover diverse languages (Python, JavaScript, Go, Rust)
- Format as (context, feedback, success_flag) tuples
  - Context: problem statement, code snapshot, architecture
  - Feedback: error messages, test results, diffs
  - Success flag: boolean for filtering
- Implement data loader with variable-length padding
- Create train/val/test splits (70/15/15)

**Deliverables**:
- Structured dataset (JSONL format)
- Data loader compatible with hypernetwork training
- Dataset statistics and quality report

**Dependencies**: None

**Estimated Time**: 3 weeks

---

## Phase 2: The Core Recursive Loop

**Objective**: Implement the minimal recursive code generation loop with hypernetwork-based adapter generation from the start.

### 2.0 Initial Project Adapter Generation

**Tasks**:
- Define project specification format
  - Goals and success criteria
  - Implementation rules (coding standards, patterns, idioms)
  - Architecture overview (layers, modules, dependencies)
  - Organizational structure (directory layout, file naming, module boundaries)
- Implement hypernetwork invocation for project initialization
  - Process specification document through trained hypernetwork
  - Generate initial project-specific adapter
  - Validate adapter improves project-relevant prompts vs base model
- Integration with recursive loop
  - Load initial adapter before first code generation
  - Ensure project context is active from iteration zero

**Deliverables**:
- Project specification schema and examples
- Initial adapter generation script
- Validation tests: project-aware vs base model comparison

**Dependencies**: 4.1 (hypernetwork), 4.2 (meta-training)

**Estimated Time**: 2 weeks

### 2.1 LoRA Training Pipeline

**Tasks**:
- Implement standard LoRA finetuning
  - Target layers: attention (Q, K, V) + MLP (down_proj)
  - Rank selection: r = 8, 16, 32, 64 (experiment)
  - LoRA alpha = 2r (standard scaling)
- Training loop with FlashOptim
  - BF16 mixed precision
  - Gradient checkpointing
  - Learning rate scheduling (cosine decay)
- Adapter export to Safetensors format
- Integration with serving layer

**Deliverables**:
- LoRA training script
- Adapter checkpoints for test tasks
- Training convergence analysis

**Dependencies**: 1.1 (base model), 1.4 (training data)

**Estimated Time**: 2 weeks

### 2.2 Recursive Generation Loop (Baseline)

**Tasks**:
- Implement loop controller
  - Generate code with current adapter
  - Submit to sandbox for execution
  - Parse execution results
  - Decide: terminate (success) or continue (max 5 iterations)
- Prompt engineering for code generation
  - System prompt with architecture context
  - Few-shot examples
  - Error-driven refinement prompts
- Logging and trajectory persistence

## Risk Mitigation

**Technical Risks**:
- **Adapter interference**: Composition may degrade performance → mitigation: Evolution Operator conflict detection, hierarchical organization
- **Hypernetwork generalization**: May fail on OOD tasks → mitigation: diverse meta-training data, continual learning
- **Catastrophic forgetting**: Adapter library grows, older adapters fail → mitigation: regression tests, archival, restoration

**Operational Risks**:
- **Hardware constraints**: 24GB VRAM insufficient → mitigation: aggressive quantization, CPU offloading, smaller base models
- **Security vulnerabilities**: Sandbox escape, prompt injection → mitigation: layered security (seccomp, network isolation, manual review)
- **Data quality**: Poor training trajectories → mitigation: manual curation initially, quality filters, success-based sampling

**Research Risks**:
- **Novel paradigm**: Parametric memory may not outperform RAG → mitigation: rigorous evaluation, compare to baselines, publish negative results
- **Evaluation metrics**: Hard to quantify memory quality → mitigation: define proxy metrics (success rate, iteration count, token efficiency)

---

## Success Metrics

**System-Level**:
- Serve 100+ adapters on 24GB GPU with <10% latency overhead
- Sub-second adapter generation via hypernetwork
- 10,000× storage reduction vs full checkpoints

**Task-Level**:
- Match or exceed base model success rate on HumanEval, MBPP
- Reduce iteration count by 30% with adapted model vs base
- >80% success rate on held-out coding tasks

**Memory Quality**:
- Adapter reuse rate >50% (adapters used in multiple tasks)
- Composition improves performance vs single adapter
- <5% catastrophic forgetting on canonical tasks

**Cost-Efficiency**:
- 90% reduction in compute vs repeated full finetuning
- Total Cost of Ownership <$1/day for continuous operation
- Competitive with frontier LLM API costs at scale
