file.content:
## Background

### Memory Approaches for Language Model Agents

Local coding agents — Aider, Cursor, Claude Code — operate within fixed context windows.
As projects grow, agents lose access to earlier interactions: patterns discovered, bugs
solved, architectural decisions made. Each new session begins without knowledge of prior
sessions. The agent re-encounters the same failure modes, re-derives the same solutions,
and cannot accumulate project-specific expertise over time.

Memory approaches for LLM agents fall into three broad categories that differ in where
and how knowledge is stored:

**Token-space memory** (retrieval-augmented generation, long-context prompting, vector
databases) retrieves text snippets and injects them into the context window before
inference. Retrieval quality determines what the agent can recall, but every retrieved
chunk consumes the same scarce resource — context tokens — that the agent needs for
new instructions and code. Scaling memory capacity in this category means scaling
context window size, which remains bounded and costly.

**Destructive weight-space memory** (model editing via ROME or MEMIT, full fine-tuning)
modifies base model weights directly. Each modification risks overwriting previously
stored knowledge — a phenomenon known as catastrophic forgetting. Knowledge is entangled
in shared parameters: storing new facts can corrupt existing capabilities because the
same weights that encode one concept participate in encoding others.

**Composable weight-space memory** (LoRA adapters, adapter libraries) stores knowledge
as discrete, composable parameter deltas that augment the base model without modifying
it. Each adapter can be loaded, removed, or replaced independently. The base model
remains a stable reference point. Multiple adapters can coexist in a library, each
encoding knowledge from a distinct session or task.

Rune occupies the composable weight-space category. Each coding trajectory is distilled
into a LoRA adapter that encodes the procedural knowledge from that session — patterns,
fixes, architectural decisions — as parameter deltas. Adapters are write-once, immutable,
and independently retrievable by task similarity.

Pink et al. argue that episodic memory — single-shot learning of instance-specific
contexts, retrievable and composable — is the missing capability for long-term LLM
agents.[^pink2025episodic] Rune's adapter-per-session design maps directly onto this
framework: each adapter is an episode, stored independently, and retrieved when a
subsequent task resembles the original.

---

### LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation

LoRA decomposes the weight update for a pre-trained weight matrix as a product of two
low-rank matrices.[^hu2021lora] Rather than updating the full weight matrix \(W_0 \in
\mathbb{R}^{d \times k}\) directly, LoRA reparameterizes the update as:

\[
\Delta W = BA, \quad B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}, \; A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}
\]

where rank \(r \ll \min(d, k)\). The trainable parameter count drops from \(dk\) to
\(r(d + k)\), which is approximately \(2rd\) when \(d \approx k\). For a 7B-parameter
model with typical hidden dimensions, rank 16–64 LoRA adapters require only a few
million parameters — small enough to store hundreds of adapters on disk and load them
on demand.

During inference, the output becomes \(h = W_0 x + BAx\), where \(W_0\) is frozen.
The adapter's contribution is additive and reversible: removing the adapter (setting
\(\Delta W = 0\)) restores the original model behavior exactly. No modification to
the base model is required.

This reversibility is what makes LoRA viable as a memory substrate rather than merely
a fine-tuning technique. Knowledge stored in one adapter does not interfere with
knowledge stored in other adapters or in the base model — each adapter is a self-contained
parameter delta that augments a stable foundation.

---

### QLoRA: Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation

QLoRA extends LoRA with 4-bit NormalFloat (NF4) quantization of the frozen base model
weights, combined with double quantization (quantizing the quantization constants
themselves) and paged optimizers for memory spill management.[^dettmers2023qlora]

The hardware relevance is significant. NF4 quantization reduces the memory footprint
of a 7B-parameter base model from approximately 14 GB (bfloat16) to approximately 4 GB,
enabling LoRA training and inference on consumer GPUs with limited VRAM. QLoRA makes it
feasible to keep the base model loaded continuously while training or serving LoRA adapters
within the VRAM budget of a typical consumer GPU.

QLoRA is an efficiency layer in Rune's design, not a core algorithmic contribution.
Rune's architecture does not depend on QLoRA specifically — any quantization scheme
that fits the base model into available VRAM while preserving LoRA training quality
would suffice. QLoRA is the practical default given current consumer hardware constraints.

---

### Multi-Adapter Serving: S-LoRA

If each coding session produces an adapter, an agent accumulating hundreds of sessions
needs a mechanism to serve the appropriate adapter at inference time without maintaining
separate model copies for each one.

S-LoRA introduces Unified Paging — a unified memory pool that manages adapter weights
and KV cache tensors together in a shared memory space, enabling thousands of concurrent
LoRA adapters to be served from a single base model instance.[^sheng2023slora] Adapters
are loaded and swapped dynamically without reloading the base model. S-LoRA also
introduces batched LoRA computation that amortizes the overhead of applying different
adapters to different requests in the same batch.

S-LoRA validates a core architectural assumption of Rune: that adapter-per-session
memory is servable at scale from a single model instance. Rune's adapter library design
— write-once adapters retrieved by task similarity — maps directly onto S-LoRA's
concurrent serving model. The base model remains loaded; the appropriate adapter is
swapped in per request based on retrieval results.

---

### Hypernetwork Architectures

Hypernetworks — networks that generate the weights of another network — were introduced
by Ha et al. as a relaxed form of weight-sharing.[^ha2016hypernetworks] Rather than
learning fixed weights, a smaller meta-network learns to produce weights conditioned
on some input signal. This decouples *what* is learned (the target network's behavior)
from *how* it is parameterized (the meta-network's learned generation function).
The paradigm enables instant adaptation: given a new conditioning input, the
hypernetwork produces a new set of weights in a single forward pass.

**Doc-to-LoRA** applies the hypernetwork paradigm to LoRA adapter generation.
A hypernetwork takes a document as input and produces LoRA adapter weights in a single
forward pass — no gradient-based fine-tuning required at inference time. The generated
adapter encodes the document's content into the base model's parameter space, enabling
needle-in-a-haystack question answering that outperforms RAG baselines on long
documents.[^charakorn2026doc2lora]

Doc-to-LoRA validates the hypernetwork-to-LoRA mechanism on textual documents and
document QA tasks — retrieving specific facts from long textual inputs. It was not
validated on coding trajectories, procedural knowledge, or code execution traces.
Whether the same mechanism can encode the richer structure of code execution
trajectories — sequential, causal, procedural — is the hypothesis Rune proposes to
test. The transfer to a structurally different input type is the core open research
question.

**SHINE** is a concurrent hypernetwork system that maps in-context examples (few-shot
demonstrations) to LoRA adapters in a single pass.[^liu2026shine] SHINE's input
modality is demonstration examples — input-output pairs the LLM would otherwise process
in-context. The hypernetwork converts this "in-context knowledge" to "in-parameter
knowledge," eliminating the context cost of few-shot prompting at inference time.
SHINE uses structured examples, not execution traces or documents — a distinction
that becomes central to the trajectory modality argument in the final subsection.

---

### LoRA Composition Methods

LoRA's additive structure (\(\Delta W = BA\)) suggests that multiple adapters could
be combined — summed, concatenated, or interpolated — to merge skills from different
training episodes into a single set of weights.

Prabhakar et al. demonstrate that concatenation of LoRA adapters (the CAT method)
outperforms data mixing for binary skill composition tasks.[^prabhakar2024lorasoups]
This is the first empirical evidence that model merging can exceed training-data-level
composition for skill acquisition — suggesting that adapters encode skills in
structures that are, at least partially, combinable.

However, composition is not straightforward. Zhang et al. show that orthogonality
between merged LoRA modules does NOT guarantee semantic disentanglement.[^zhang2025orthogonality]
Enforcing orthogonal adapter subspaces prevents direct parameter interference, but
cannot ensure that composed adapters produce semantically coherent behavior. Two
adapters can occupy orthogonal subspaces yet still produce conflicting behavioral
outputs when applied together.

Additional evidence from Zou demonstrates that merging adapters can cause unwanted
re-emergence of latent reasoning traces due to partially misaligned update
directions.[^zou2026merging] Composition can reactivate behaviors from individual
adapters that were not intended to surface in the merged result.

These interference results inform Rune's design choice: the default retrieval mode
is single-adapter selection — the most relevant adapter loaded alone, not composed
with others. Multi-adapter composition is an experimental extension. Rune's Evolution
Operator (described in Methods) addresses composition through fitness-driven selection
rather than naive merging, allowing the system to discover which adapter combinations
produce beneficial behavior rather than assuming composition is safe by default.

---

### Programming by Backprop: Code as Procedural Abstraction

Cook et al. demonstrate that declarative instructions — including code — can substitute
for up to 100 execution examples when fine-tuning LLMs.[^cook2025pbb] A single
well-structured instruction achieves comparable weight updates to training on dozens
of input-output pairs. This result establishes that the format of training signal
matters: procedural, instruction-like representations are more information-dense than
equivalent example sets.

This finding provides independent empirical support for Rune's core premise: that code
execution trajectories — structured, procedural, and instruction-like — may be an
efficient substrate for adapter training. If instructions can substitute for examples,
then trajectories, which contain both the instructions (the code) and the procedural
context (execution results, error messages, corrections), may encode knowledge more
efficiently than input-output pair datasets of equivalent size.

PBB also finds, however, that in-context instruction execution remains more reliable
than weight-based procedural knowledge for the tasks they tested. This means the
approach is promising but not guaranteed: there is a gap between what can be encoded
in weights via backpropagation and what can be reliably executed from those weights.
Rune must empirically validate whether trajectory-trained adapters close this gap for
coding tasks specifically.

---

### Concurrent Hypernetwork Work and the Trajectory Modality Argument

Multiple hypernetwork systems now exist for generating LoRA adapters from different
input types. The key differentiator across these systems is the input modality — what
goes in determines what kind of knowledge is encoded. Rune's proposed input modality,
code execution trajectories, is structurally distinct from all existing systems.

| System | Input Modality | Knowledge Encoded |
|--------|---------------|-------------------|
| Text-to-LoRA (Charakorn et al., 2025)[^charakorn2025t2l] | Natural language task descriptions | Declarative task specification — static, instructional |
| Doc-to-LoRA (Charakorn et al., 2026) | Long documents | Factual content — informational, unstructured |
| SHINE (Liu et al., 2026) | In-context examples (few-shot demos) | Demonstrated patterns — exemplary, structured pairs |
| Rune (proposed) | Code execution trajectories | Procedural knowledge — sequential, causal, self-correcting |

Code execution trajectories differ from the other three modalities in three structural
ways:

**Temporal ordering.** Steps in a trajectory have a meaningful causal sequence: code
generation precedes execution, execution precedes error, error precedes diagnosis,
diagnosis precedes fix. This temporal structure carries information about causality
that is absent from documents (which present facts without an ordering obligation)
and from examples (which present correct input-output pairs without the process that
generated them).

**Procedural abstraction.** A trajectory encodes a procedure — how to solve a problem
step by step — rather than a fact (what is true), an instruction (what to do), or an
example (what a correct pair looks like). Procedural knowledge is action-oriented:
it specifies operations, their preconditions, and their effects.

**Self-correcting structure.** Trajectories naturally contain failure-recovery patterns:
attempt → error → diagnosis → fix. These patterns are absent from documents, instructions,
and demonstrations. A trajectory that includes a bug and its correction encodes both
the error pattern and its resolution in the same training signal.

PBB's finding that instructions substitute for examples supports this argument from an
independent direction: if procedural representations are efficient for weight-based
learning, then trajectories — which are the richest procedural representation, containing
both the procedure and its full execution context — may be the most efficient input
modality for hypernetwork-based adapter generation. Whether this theoretical efficiency
advantage holds in practice is a central empirical question for Rune. PBB validated
instructions on a different task distribution; trajectories have not yet been tested
as hypernetwork input.

---

[^pink2025episodic]: Pink, M., et al. (2025). Position: Episodic Memory is the Missing Piece for Long-Term LLM Agents. *arXiv:2502.06975*. [Full entry](references.md#pink2025episodic)

1→"""Trajectory recording and formatting for coding session distillation.
     2→
     3→Provides functions to persist, load, and convert coding session trajectories
     4→into SFT-compatible chat format for LoRA fine-tuning pipelines.
     5→"""
     6→
     7→from __future__ import annotations
     8→
     9→import json
    10→import os
    11→from datetime import datetime, timezone
    12→from pathlib import Path

"""Trajectory recording and formatting for coding session distillation.

Provides functions to persist, load, and convert coding session trajectories
into SFT-compatible chat format for LoRA fine-tuning pipelines.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import json
import os
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any, Optional

SYSTEM_PROMPT = "You are a Python code generator. Output only code, no explanation."


def _get_trajectory_dir() -> Path:
    """Return the trajectory storage directory, respecting RUNE_TRAJECTORY_DIR env var.

    Reads env var inside function body (not module level) so that monkeypatch.setenv()
    works correctly in tests.
    """
    env_dir = os.environ.get("RUNE_TRAJECTORY_DIR")
    if env_dir:
        return Path(env_dir)
    return Path.home() / ".rune" / "trajectories"


def record_trajectory(
    session_id: str,
    steps: list[dict[str, Any]],
    outcome: Optional[str] = None,
    *,
    task_description: str = "",
    task_type: str = "",
    adapter_ids: list[str] | None = None,
) -> dict[str, Any]:
    """Persist a coding session trajectory to disk for future distillation.

    Args:
        session_id: Unique identifier for the coding session.
        steps: List of step dicts, each containing attempt results.
        outcome: Final session result ('success', 'exhausted', or None).
        task_description: Natural language description of the coding task.
        task_type: Category of task (e.g. 'function', 'class', 'refactor').
        adapter_ids: LoRA adapter IDs used during the session.

    Returns:
        A dict with 'session_id' and 'file_path' keys.
    """
    trajectory_dir = _get_trajectory_dir()
    trajectory_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    file_path = trajectory_dir / f"{session_id}.json"
    timestamp = datetime.now(tz=timezone.utc).isoformat()

    trajectory: dict[str, Any] = {
        "session_id": session_id,
        "task_description": task_description,
        "task_type": task_type,
        "adapter_ids": adapter_ids if adapter_ids is not None else [],
        "outcome": outcome,
        "timestamp": timestamp,
        "steps": steps,
    }

    file_path.write_text(json.dumps(trajectory, indent=2))

    return {"session_id": session_id, "file_path": str(file_path)}


def load_trajectory(trajectory_id: str) -> dict[str, Any]:
    """Load a stored trajectory by session ID.

    Args:
        trajectory_id: The session ID used as the filename (without .json).

    Returns:
        A dict containing the full trajectory data including steps and metadata.

    Raises:
        FileNotFoundError: If no trajectory file exists for the given ID.
    """
    trajectory_dir = _get_trajectory_dir()
    file_path = trajectory_dir / f"{trajectory_id}.json"
    # Let FileNotFoundError propagate naturally if file does not exist
    return json.loads(file_path.read_text())  # type: ignore[no-any-return]


def format_for_sft(trajectory: dict[str, Any]) -> list[dict[str, str]]:
    """Convert a trajectory into SFT-compatible chat format.

    Only successful trajectories (outcome == 'success') produce output.
    Extracts the final step where tests_passed is True as the assistant message.

    Args:
        trajectory: A trajectory dict as returned by load_trajectory.

    Returns:
        A list of 3 message dicts ([system, user, assistant]) for successful
        trajectories, or an empty list if the trajectory did not succeed.
    """
    if trajectory.get("outcome") != "success":
        return []

    steps: list[dict[str, Any]] = trajectory.get("steps", [])
    successful_step = next(
        (s for s in reversed(steps) if s.get("tests_passed")),
        None,
    )

    if successful_step is None:
        return []

    task_description: str = trajectory.get("task_description", "")
    generated_code: str = successful_step.get("generated_code", "")

    return [
        {"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM_PROMPT},
        {"role": "user", "content": task_description},
        {"role": "assistant", "content": generated_code},
    ]

1→"""DocToLoraHypernetwork: Perceiver-based instant LoRA adapter generation.
     2→
     3→Generates rank-8 LoRA adapter weights from token IDs in a single forward pass.
     4→Distinct from the QLoRA gradient-descent path (Phase 21) — this produces adapters
     5→in <1s by cross-attending over token embeddings with learned latents.
     6→
     7→IMPORTANT: All GPU imports (torch, safetensors) are deferred inside function/method
     8→bodies per INFRA-05 pattern — this module is importable in CPU-only CI.
     9→
    10→Usage:
    11→    from model_training.hypernetwork import (
    12→        DocToLoraHypernetwork,
    13→        save_hypernetwork_adapter,
    14→    )
    15→
    16→    model = DocToLoraHypernetwork(input_dim=32000)
    17→    weights = model(token_ids)
    18→    save_hypernetwork_adapter(weights, "/tmp/adapter", "Qwen/Qwen2.5-Coder-7B")
    19→"""
    20→
    21→from __future__ import annotations
    22→
    23→import json
    24→from pathlib import Path
    25→from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, Any, Sequence
    26→
    27→if TYPE_CHECKING:
    28→    import torch
    29→
    30→
    31→def _build_hypernetwork_class() -> type:
    32→    """Build and return DocToLoraHypernetwork as a real nn.Module subclass.
    33→
    34→    This function is called the first time DocToLoraHypernetwork is instantiated
    35→    so that torch.nn.Module is only imported when needed (INFRA-05 pattern).
    36→    """
    37→    import torch  # noqa: PLC0415
    38→    import torch.nn as nn  # noqa: PLC0415
    39→
    40→    class _DocToLoraHypernetwork(nn.Module):
    41→        """Perceiver-based hypernetwork that generates LoRA adapter weights.
    42→
    43→        Given a sequence of token IDs (from a document/trajectory), produces a
    44→        PEFT-compatible state_dict with lora_A and lora_B matrices for every
    45→        target_module across all transformer layers — in a single forward pass.
    46→
    47→        Architecture:
    48→        - Lightweight learned token embedding (not the 7B base model's embedding)
    49→        - Learnable latent array (num_latents x latent_dim)
    50→        - Cross-attention: latents attend over token embeddings
    51→        - Self-attention stack: process latents
    52→        - Linear head: project to all LoRA weight parameters
    53→
    54→        Args:
    55→            input_dim: Vocabulary size for the hypernetwork's own token embedding.
    56→            num_latents: Number of learnable latent vectors. Default: 32.
    57→            latent_dim: Dimensionality of each latent vector. Default: 256.
    58→            depth: Number of self-attention layers over latents. Default: 4.
    59→            heads: Number of attention heads. Default: 8.
    60→            rank: LoRA rank for generated adapters. Default: 8.
    61→            target_modules: LoRA target module names. Default: ("q_proj", "v_proj").
    62→            num_layers: Number of transformer layers in target model. Default: 28.
    63→            hidden_dim: Hidden dim of q_proj/v_proj in target model. Default: 4096.
    64→        """
    65→
    66→        def __init__(
    67→            self,
    68→            input_dim: int,
    69→            num_latents: int = 32,
    70→            latent_dim: int = 256,
    71→            depth: int = 4,
    72→            heads: int = 8,
    73→            rank: int = 8,
    74→            target_modules: Sequence[str] = ("q_proj", "v_proj"),
    75→            num_layers: int = 28,
    76→            hidden_dim: int = 4096,
    77→        ) -> None:
    78→            super().__init__()
    79→
    80→            self.rank = rank
    81→            self.hidden_dim = hidden_dim
    82→            self.target_modules: list[str] = list(target_modules)
    83→            self.num_layers = num_layers
    84→            self.num_latents = num_latents
    85→            self.latent_dim = latent_dim
    86→
    87→            # Hypernetwork's own lightweight embedding — does NOT load the 7B model
    88→            self.token_embedding = nn.Embedding(input_dim, latent_dim)
    89→
    90→            # Learnable latent array: shape (num_latents, latent_dim)
    91→            self.latents = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(num_latents, latent_dim))
    92→
    93→            # Cross-attention: latents (Q) attend over token embeddings (K, V)
    94→            self.cross_attend = nn.MultiheadAttention(
    95→                latent_dim, heads, batch_first=True
    96→            )
    97→
    98→            # Self-attention stack to process latents
    99→            encoder_layer = nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(
   100→                latent_dim, heads, batch_first=True
   101→            )
   102→            self.self_attend = nn.TransformerEncoder(encoder_layer, num_layers=depth)
   103→
   104→            # Project flattened latents to all LoRA weight parameters.
   105→            # Each (layer, module) needs:
   106→            #   lora_A: rank * hidden_dim params
   107→            #   lora_B: hidden_dim * rank params
   108→            total_params = len(target_modules) * num_layers * 2 * rank * hidden_dim
   109→            self.weight_head = nn.Linear(latent_dim * num_latents, total_params)
   110→
   111→        def forward(self, token_ids: torch.Tensor) -> dict[str, torch.Tensor]:
   112→            """Generate PEFT-compatible LoRA adapter weights from token IDs.
   113→
   114→            Args:
   115→                token_ids: Integer token ID tensor of shape (batch, seq_len).
   116→
   117→            Returns:
   118→                Dict mapping PEFT state_dict keys to weight tensors.
   119→                Keys follow:
   120→                  base_model.model.model.layers.{i}.self_attn.{module}.lora_{A|B}.weight
   121→                Returns first batch element (one adapter per trajectory).
   122→            """
   123→            batch_size = token_ids.shape[0]
   124→
   125→            # Embed token IDs with hypernetwork's own lightweight embedding
   126→            x = self.token_embedding(token_ids)  # (batch, seq_len, latent_dim)
   127→
   128→            # Expand latents for batch dimension
   129→            latents = self.latents.unsqueeze(0).expand(
   130→                batch_size, -1, -1
   131→            )  # (batch, num_latents, latent_dim)
   132→
   133→            # Cross-attention: latents attend over token embeddings
   134→            latents, _ = self.cross_attend(
   135→                latents, x, x
   136→            )  # (batch, num_latents, latent_dim)
   137→
   138→            # Self-attention stack over latents
   139→            latents = self.self_attend(latents)  # (batch, num_latents, latent_dim)
   140→
   141→            # Flatten latents
   142→            latents = latents.reshape(
   143→                batch_size, -1
   144→            )  # (batch, num_latents * latent_dim)
   145→
   146→            # Project to all LoRA weight parameters
   147→            weights = self.weight_head(latents)  # (batch, total_weight_params)
   148→
   149→            # Reshape into PEFT state_dict for first batch element (index 0)
   150→            state_dict: dict[str, torch.Tensor] = {}
   151→            offset = 0
   152→            a_size = self.rank * self.hidden_dim
   153→            b_size = self.hidden_dim * self.rank
   154→
   155→            for i in range(self.num_layers):
   156→                for module in self.target_modules:
   157→                    # lora_A: shape (rank, hidden_dim)
   158→                    key_a = (
   159→                        f"base_model.model.model.layers.{i}"
   160→                        f".self_attn.{module}.lora_A.weight"
   161→                    )
   162→                    state_dict[key_a] = weights[0, offset : offset + a_size].reshape(
   163→                        self.rank, self.hidden_dim
   164→                    )
   165→                    offset += a_size
   166→
   167→                    # lora_B: shape (hidden_dim, rank)
   168→                    key_b = (
   169→                        f"base_model.model.model.layers.{i}"
   170→                        f".self_attn.{module}.lora_B.weight"
   171→                    )
   172→                    state_dict[key_b] = weights[0, offset : offset + b_size].reshape(
   173→                        self.hidden_dim, self.rank
   174→                    )
   175→                    offset += b_size
   176→
   177→            return state_dict
   178→
   179→    return _DocToLoraHypernetwork
   180→
   181→
   182→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   183→# DocToLoraHypernetwork: lazy proxy that builds the real nn.Module class on
   184→# first instantiation. This keeps the module importable without torch.
   185→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   186→
   187→
   188→class _LazyHypernetworkProxy:
   189→    """Lazy proxy for DocToLoraHypernetwork.
   190→
   191→    Acts as a callable that creates a real nn.Module instance on first call.
   192→    The class is rebuilt (and cached) on first instantiation so that torch is
   193→    only imported when actually needed (INFRA-05 pattern).
   194→    """
   195→
   196→    _real_class: type | None = None
   197→
   198→    def __call__(self, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> Any:
   199→        if self._real_class is None:
   200→            self.__class__._real_class = _build_hypernetwork_class()
   201→        return self._real_class(*args, **kwargs)  # type: ignore[misc]
   202→
   203→    def __instancecheck__(self, instance: object) -> bool:
   204→        if self._real_class is None:
   205→            return False
   206→        return isinstance(instance, self._real_class)
   207→
   208→
   209→DocToLoraHypernetwork: _LazyHypernetworkProxy = _LazyHypernetworkProxy()
   210→
   211→
   212→def save_hypernetwork_adapter(
   213→    weights: dict[str, "torch.Tensor"],
   214→    output_dir: str,
   215→    base_model_id: str,
   216→    rank: int = 8,
   217→    target_modules: list[str] | None = None,
   218→) -> None:
   219→    """Serialize hypernetwork-generated LoRA weights in PEFT adapter format.
   220→
   221→    Writes:
   222→    - adapter_model.safetensors: the LoRA weight tensors
   223→    - adapter_config.json: PEFT-compatible configuration
   224→
   225→    Args:
   226→        weights: PEFT state_dict from DocToLoraHypernetwork.forward().
   227→        output_dir: Directory to write adapter files to (created if needed).
   228→        base_model_id: HuggingFace model ID of the base model.
   229→        rank: LoRA rank. Default: 8.
   230→        target_modules: List of module names. Default: ["q_proj", "v_proj"].
   231→
   232→    Note:
   233→        Does NOT include embed_tokens or lm_head — vLLM rejects these in adapters
   234→        (per Phase 21-01 decision: no modules_to_save in LoraConfig).
   235→    """
   236→    from safetensors.torch import save_file  # noqa: PLC0415
   237→
   238→    if target_modules is None:
   239→        target_modules = ["q_proj", "v_proj"]
   240→
   241→    output_path = Path(output_dir)
   242→    output_path.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
   243→
   244→    # Write adapter weights as safetensors
   245→    safetensors_path = output_path / "adapter_model.safetensors"
   246→    save_file(weights, str(safetensors_path))
   247→
   248→    # Write PEFT-compatible adapter_config.json
   249→    adapter_config: dict[str, object] = {
   250→        "peft_type": "LORA",
   251→        "r": rank,
   252→        "lora_alpha": rank * 2,
   253→        "target_modules": target_modules,
   254→        "lora_dropout": 0.0,
   255→        "bias": "none",
   256→        "task_type": "CAUSAL_LM",
   257→        "base_model_name_or_path": base_model_id,
   258→        "inference_mode": True,
   259→        "modules_to_save": None,
   260→        "fan_in_fan_out": False,
   261→    }
   262→
   263→    config_path = output_path / "adapter_config.json"
   264→    config_path.write_text(json.dumps(adapter_config, indent=2))
   265→

"""DocToLoraHypernetwork: Perceiver-based instant LoRA adapter generation.

Generates rank-8 LoRA adapter weights from token IDs in a single forward pass.
Distinct from the QLoRA gradient-descent path (Phase 21) — this produces adapters
in <1s by cross-attending over token embeddings with learned latents.

IMPORTANT: All GPU imports (torch, safetensors) are deferred inside function/method
bodies per INFRA-05 pattern — this module is importable in CPU-only CI.

Usage:
    from model_training.hypernetwork import (
        DocToLoraHypernetwork,
        save_hypernetwork_adapter,
    )

    model = DocToLoraHypernetwork(input_dim=32000)
    weights = model(token_ids)
    save_hypernetwork_adapter(weights, "/tmp/adapter", "Qwen/Qwen2.5-Coder-7B")
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import json
from pathlib import Path
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, Any, Sequence

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    import torch


def _build_hypernetwork_class() -> type:
    """Build and return DocToLoraHypernetwork as a real nn.Module subclass.

    This function is called the first time DocToLoraHypernetwork is instantiated
    so that torch.nn.Module is only imported when needed (INFRA-05 pattern).
    """
    import torch  # noqa: PLC0415
    import torch.nn as nn  # noqa: PLC0415

    class _DocToLoraHypernetwork(nn.Module):
        """Perceiver-based hypernetwork that generates LoRA adapter weights.

        Given a sequence of token IDs (from a document/trajectory), produces a
        PEFT-compatible state_dict with lora_A and lora_B matrices for every
        target_module across all transformer layers — in a single forward pass.

        Architecture:
        - Lightweight learned token embedding (not the 7B base model's embedding)
        - Learnable latent array (num_latents x latent_dim)
        - Cross-attention: latents attend over token embeddings
        - Self-attention stack: process latents
        - Linear head: project to all LoRA weight parameters

        Args:
            input_dim: Vocabulary size for the hypernetwork's own token embedding.
            num_latents: Number of learnable latent vectors. Default: 32.
            latent_dim: Dimensionality of each latent vector. Default: 256.
            depth: Number of self-attention layers over latents. Default: 4.
            heads: Number of attention heads. Default: 8.
            rank: LoRA rank for generated adapters. Default: 8.
            target_modules: LoRA target module names. Default: ("q_proj", "v_proj").
            num_layers: Number of transformer layers in target model. Default: 28.
            hidden_dim: Hidden dim of q_proj/v_proj in target model. Default: 4096.
        """

        def __init__(
            self,
            input_dim: int,
            num_latents: int = 32,
            latent_dim: int = 256,
            depth: int = 4,
            heads: int = 8,
            rank: int = 8,
            target_modules: Sequence[str] = ("q_proj", "v_proj"),
            num_layers: int = 28,
            hidden_dim: int = 4096,
        ) -> None:
            super().__init__()

            self.rank = rank
            self.hidden_dim = hidden_dim
            self.target_modules: list[str] = list(target_modules)
            self.num_layers = num_layers
            self.num_latents = num_latents
            self.latent_dim = latent_dim

            # Hypernetwork's own lightweight embedding — does NOT load the 7B model
            self.token_embedding = nn.Embedding(input_dim, latent_dim)

            # Learnable latent array: shape (num_latents, latent_dim)
            self.latents = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(num_latents, latent_dim))

            # Cross-attention: latents (Q) attend over token embeddings (K, V)
            self.cross_attend = nn.MultiheadAttention(
                latent_dim, heads, batch_first=True
            )

            # Self-attention stack to process latents
            encoder_layer = nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(
                latent_dim, heads, batch_first=True
            )
            self.self_attend = nn.TransformerEncoder(encoder_layer, num_layers=depth)

            # Project flattened latents to all LoRA weight parameters.
            # Each (layer, module) needs:
            #   lora_A: rank * hidden_dim params
            #   lora_B: hidden_dim * rank params
            total_params = len(target_modules) * num_layers * 2 * rank * hidden_dim
            self.weight_head = nn.Linear(latent_dim * num_latents, total_params)

        def forward(self, token_ids: torch.Tensor) -> dict[str, torch.Tensor]:
            """Generate PEFT-compatible LoRA adapter weights from token IDs.

            Args:
                token_ids: Integer token ID tensor of shape (batch, seq_len).

            Returns:
                Dict mapping PEFT state_dict keys to weight tensors.
                Keys follow:
                  base_model.model.model.layers.{i}.self_attn.{module}.lora_{A|B}.weight
                Returns first batch element (one adapter per trajectory).
            """
            batch_size = token_ids.shape[0]

            # Embed token IDs with hypernetwork's own lightweight embedding
            x = self.token_embedding(token_ids)  # (batch, seq_len, latent_dim)

            # Expand latents for batch dimension
            latents = self.latents.unsqueeze(0).expand(
                batch_size, -1, -1
            )  # (batch, num_latents, latent_dim)

            # Cross-attention: latents attend over token embeddings
            latents, _ = self.cross_attend(
                latents, x, x
            )  # (batch, num_latents, latent_dim)

            # Self-attention stack over latents
            latents = self.self_attend(latents)  # (batch, num_latents, latent_dim)

            # Flatten latents
            latents = latents.reshape(
                batch_size, -1
            )  # (batch, num_latents * latent_dim)

            # Project to all LoRA weight parameters
            weights = self.weight_head(latents)  # (batch, total_weight_params)

            # Reshape into PEFT state_dict for first batch element (index 0)
            state_dict: dict[str, torch.Tensor] = {}
            offset = 0
            a_size = self.rank * self.hidden_dim
            b_size = self.hidden_dim * self.rank

            for i in range(self.num_layers):
                for module in self.target_modules:
                    # lora_A: shape (rank, hidden_dim)
                    key_a = (
                        f"base_model.model.model.layers.{i}"
                        f".self_attn.{module}.lora_A.weight"
                    )
                    state_dict[key_a] = weights[0, offset : offset + a_size].reshape(
                        self.rank, self.hidden_dim
                    )
                    offset += a_size

                    # lora_B: shape (hidden_dim, rank)
                    key_b = (
                        f"base_model.model.model.layers.{i}"
                        f".self_attn.{module}.lora_B.weight"
                    )
                    state_dict[key_b] = weights[0, offset : offset + b_size].reshape(
                        self.hidden_dim, self.rank
                    )
                    offset += b_size

            return state_dict

    return _DocToLoraHypernetwork


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# DocToLoraHypernetwork: lazy proxy that builds the real nn.Module class on
# first instantiation. This keeps the module importable without torch.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------


class _LazyHypernetworkProxy:
    """Lazy proxy for DocToLoraHypernetwork.

    Acts as a callable that creates a real nn.Module instance on first call.
    The class is rebuilt (and cached) on first instantiation so that torch is
    only imported when actually needed (INFRA-05 pattern).
    """

    _real_class: type | None = None

    def __call__(self, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> Any:
        if self._real_class is None:
            self.__class__._real_class = _build_hypernetwork_class()
        return self._real_class(*args, **kwargs)  # type: ignore[misc]

    def __instancecheck__(self, instance: object) -> bool:
        if self._real_class is None:
            return False
        return isinstance(instance, self._real_class)


DocToLoraHypernetwork: _LazyHypernetworkProxy = _LazyHypernetworkProxy()


def save_hypernetwork_adapter(
    weights: dict[str, "torch.Tensor"],
    output_dir: str,
    base_model_id: str,
    rank: int = 8,
    target_modules: list[str] | None = None,
) -> None:
    """Serialize hypernetwork-generated LoRA weights in PEFT adapter format.

    Writes:
    - adapter_model.safetensors: the LoRA weight tensors
    - adapter_config.json: PEFT-compatible configuration

    Args:
        weights: PEFT state_dict from DocToLoraHypernetwork.forward().
        output_dir: Directory to write adapter files to (created if needed).
        base_model_id: HuggingFace model ID of the base model.
        rank: LoRA rank. Default: 8.
        target_modules: List of module names. Default: ["q_proj", "v_proj"].

    Note:
        Does NOT include embed_tokens or lm_head — vLLM rejects these in adapters
        (per Phase 21-01 decision: no modules_to_save in LoraConfig).
    """
    from safetensors.torch import save_file  # noqa: PLC0415

    if target_modules is None:
        target_modules = ["q_proj", "v_proj"]

    output_path = Path(output_dir)
    output_path.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    # Write adapter weights as safetensors
    safetensors_path = output_path / "adapter_model.safetensors"
    save_file(weights, str(safetensors_path))

    # Write PEFT-compatible adapter_config.json
    adapter_config: dict[str, object] = {
        "peft_type": "LORA",
        "r": rank,
        "lora_alpha": rank * 2,
        "target_modules": target_modules,
        "lora_dropout": 0.0,
        "bias": "none",
        "task_type": "CAUSAL_LM",
        "base_model_name_or_path": base_model_id,
        "inference_mode": True,
        "modules_to_save": None,
        "fan_in_fan_out": False,
    }

    config_path = output_path / "adapter_config.json"
    config_path.write_text(json.dumps(adapter_config, indent=2))

"""QLoRA training orchestrator.

All GPU-dependent imports (datasets, transformers, trl, torch) are deferred
inside function bodies to ensure CPU-only importability (INFRA-05).

Module-level imports: stdlib only.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import hashlib
import os
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path


def train_qlora(
    session_id: str,
    adapter_id: str,
    output_dir: str,
    *,
    base_model_id: str | None = None,
    task_type: str = "code-gen",
    rank: int = 64,
    alpha: int = 128,
    epochs: int = 3,
    learning_rate: float = 2e-4,
) -> str:
    """Train a QLoRA adapter from a recorded coding trajectory.

    Orchestrates the full training pipeline: load trajectory, format as SFT
    messages, build dataset, load model with NF4 quantization, train with SFT,
    and save the adapter to output_dir.

    All GPU imports are deferred to this function body; the module is safe to
    import in CPU-only environments.

    Args:
        session_id: Trajectory session ID to train from.
        adapter_id: Unique identifier for the resulting adapter.
        output_dir: Directory to save the trained adapter weights.
        base_model_id: HuggingFace model ID. Defaults to RUNE_BASE_MODEL env var
            or "Qwen/Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct".
        task_type: Task category (e.g. 'code-gen', 'bug-fix').
        rank: LoRA rank.
        alpha: LoRA alpha scaling factor.
        epochs: Number of training epochs.
        learning_rate: Optimizer learning rate.

    Returns:
        output_dir path where the adapter was saved.

    Raises:
        FileNotFoundError: If the trajectory file does not exist.
        ValueError: If the trajectory is not successful or has no SFT messages.
    """
    # Deferred GPU imports — all in function body for CPU-only importability
    import torch
    from datasets import Dataset
    from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, BitsAndBytesConfig
    from trl import SFTConfig, SFTTrainer

    from model_training.peft_utils import build_qlora_config
    from model_training.trajectory import format_for_sft, load_trajectory

    # Resolve model ID — read env var inside function body for monkeypatch testability
    model_id = base_model_id or os.environ.get(
        "RUNE_BASE_MODEL", "Qwen/Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct"
    )

    # Load and format trajectory
    trajectory = load_trajectory(session_id)  # raises FileNotFoundError if missing
    messages = format_for_sft(trajectory)
    if not messages:
        raise ValueError(
            f"Trajectory {session_id} is not successful or has no SFT messages"
        )

    # Build dataset
    dataset = Dataset.from_list([{"messages": messages}])

    # NF4 quantization config (bfloat16 compute dtype prevents silent NaN loss)
    bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
        load_in_4bit=True,
        bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",
        bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
        bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,
    )

    # Load model and tokenizer
    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
        model_id,
        quantization_config=bnb_config,
        device_map="auto",
    )
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)

    # Build LoRA config (bfloat16 is set in BitsAndBytesConfig, not LoraConfig)
    lora_config = build_qlora_config(
        rank=rank,
        alpha=alpha,
        target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj"],
        dropout=0.1,
    )

    # Training arguments
    training_args = SFTConfig(
        output_dir=output_dir,
        num_train_epochs=epochs,
        learning_rate=learning_rate,
        warmup_ratio=0.03,
        lr_scheduler_type="cosine",
        bf16=True,
        per_device_train_batch_size=1,
        gradient_accumulation_steps=4,
        save_strategy="no",
        logging_steps=1,
        report_to="none",
        eval_strategy="no",
    )

    # Create and run trainer
    trainer = SFTTrainer(
        model=model,
        args=training_args,
        train_dataset=dataset,
        peft_config=lora_config,
        processing_class=tokenizer,
    )
    trainer.train()

    # Save adapter weights only (PEFT-aware: safetensors + adapter_config.json)
    trainer.save_model(output_dir)

    return output_dir


def train_and_register(
    session_id: str,
    adapter_id: str,
    *,
    base_model_id: str | None = None,
    task_type: str = "code-gen",
    rank: int = 64,
    alpha: int = 128,
    epochs: int = 3,
    learning_rate: float = 2e-4,
    database_url: str | None = None,
) -> str:
    """Train a QLoRA adapter and register it in the AdapterRegistry.

    Combines train_qlora() with AdapterRegistry.store() to produce a fully
    registered adapter ready for vLLM serving.

    Args:
        session_id: Trajectory session ID to train from.
        adapter_id: Unique identifier for the resulting adapter.
        base_model_id: HuggingFace model ID. Defaults to RUNE_BASE_MODEL env var.
        task_type: Task category (e.g. 'code-gen', 'bug-fix').
        rank: LoRA rank.
        alpha: LoRA alpha scaling factor.
        epochs: Number of training epochs.
        learning_rate: Optimizer learning rate.
        database_url: SQLAlchemy database URL. Defaults to RUNE_DATABASE_URL env var
            or "sqlite:///{home}/.rune/rune.db".

    Returns:
        adapter_id of the registered adapter.

    Raises:
        FileNotFoundError: If the trajectory file does not exist.
        ValueError: If the trajectory is not successful or has no SFT messages.
    """
    from adapter_registry.models import AdapterRecord
    from adapter_registry.registry import AdapterRegistry
    from sqlalchemy import create_engine

    # Resolve adapter output dir — read env var inside function body
    adapter_base = os.environ.get("RUNE_ADAPTER_DIR")
    if adapter_base:
        adapter_dir = Path(adapter_base) / adapter_id
    else:
        adapter_dir = Path.home() / ".rune" / "adapters" / adapter_id
    adapter_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    # Resolve model ID inside function body for testability
    model_id = base_model_id or os.environ.get(
        "RUNE_BASE_MODEL", "Qwen/Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct"
    )

    output_dir = str(adapter_dir)
    train_qlora(
        session_id=session_id,
        adapter_id=adapter_id,
        output_dir=output_dir,
        base_model_id=model_id,
        task_type=task_type,
        rank=rank,
        alpha=alpha,
        epochs=epochs,
        learning_rate=learning_rate,
    )

    # Compute file hash and size from the saved safetensors file
    adapter_file = adapter_dir / "adapter_model.safetensors"
    file_hash = hashlib.sha256(adapter_file.read_bytes()).hexdigest()
    file_size_bytes = adapter_file.stat().st_size

    # Build the adapter record
    record = AdapterRecord(
        id=adapter_id,
        version=1,
        task_type=task_type,
        base_model_id=model_id,
        rank=rank,
        created_at=datetime.now(tz=timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        file_path=output_dir,
        file_hash=file_hash,
        file_size_bytes=file_size_bytes,
        source="qlora",
        session_id=session_id,
    )

    # Resolve DB URL inside function body for testability
    db_url = database_url or os.environ.get(
        "RUNE_DATABASE_URL",
        f"sqlite:///{Path.home() / '.rune' / 'rune.db'}",
    )
    engine = create_engine(db_url)
    registry = AdapterRegistry(engine=engine)
    registry.store(record)

    return adapter_id

1→"""QLoRA PEFT configuration and adapter management.
     2→
     3→All GPU library imports (peft, transformers, torch) are deferred inside
     4→function bodies to ensure CPU-only importability (INFRA-05).
     5→"""
     6→
     7→from __future__ import annotations
     8→
     9→from typing import Any
    10→
    11→
    12→def build_qlora_config(
    13→    rank: int,
    14→    alpha: int,
    15→    target_modules: list[str],
    16→    dropout: float = 0.1,
    17→) -> Any:
    18→    """Build a QLoRA configuration for PEFT fine-tuning.
    19→
    20→    Args:
    21→        rank: LoRA rank (dimensionality of low-rank matrices).
    22→        alpha: LoRA alpha scaling factor.
    23→        target_modules: List of module names to apply LoRA to.
    24→        dropout: Dropout probability for LoRA layers.
    25→
    26→    Returns:
    27→        A peft LoraConfig instance configured for QLoRA.
    28→
    29→    Example:
    30→        >>> config = build_qlora_config(rank=64, alpha=128, target_modules=["q_proj"])
    31→    """
    32→    from peft import LoraConfig  # deferred — GPU/peft not available in CPU CI
    33→
    34→    return LoraConfig(
    35→        r=rank,
    36→        lora_alpha=alpha,
    37→        target_modules=target_modules,
    38→        lora_dropout=dropout,
    39→        bias="none",
    40→        task_type="CAUSAL_LM",
    41→    )
    42→
    43→

"""QLoRA PEFT configuration and adapter management.

All GPU library imports (peft, transformers, torch) are deferred inside
function bodies to ensure CPU-only importability (INFRA-05).
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from typing import Any


def build_qlora_config(
    rank: int,
    alpha: int,
    target_modules: list[str],
    dropout: float = 0.1,
) -> Any:
    """Build a QLoRA configuration for PEFT fine-tuning.

    Args:
        rank: LoRA rank (dimensionality of low-rank matrices).
        alpha: LoRA alpha scaling factor.
        target_modules: List of module names to apply LoRA to.
        dropout: Dropout probability for LoRA layers.

    Returns:
        A peft LoraConfig instance configured for QLoRA.

    Example:
        >>> config = build_qlora_config(rank=64, alpha=128, target_modules=["q_proj"])
    """
    from peft import LoraConfig  # deferred — GPU/peft not available in CPU CI

    return LoraConfig(
        r=rank,
        lora_alpha=alpha,
        target_modules=target_modules,
        lora_dropout=dropout,
        bias="none",
        task_type="CAUSAL_LM",
    )


def apply_lora_adapter(model: Any, config: Any) -> Any:
    """Apply a LoRA adapter to a base model.

    Args:
        model: The base model to wrap with LoRA.
        config: The LoRA configuration (from build_qlora_config).

    Returns:
        The model wrapped with a LoRA adapter via peft.get_peft_model.

    Example:
        >>> adapted_model = apply_lora_adapter(base_model, lora_config)
    """
    from peft import get_peft_model  # deferred — GPU/peft not available in CPU CI

    return get_peft_model(model, config)


def merge_adapter(model: Any) -> Any:
    """Merge LoRA weights into the base model.

    Args:
        model: A PEFT model with LoRA adapter applied.

    Returns:
        The base model with LoRA weights merged in.

    Raises:
        NotImplementedError: Adapter merging is out of scope for Phase 21.

    Example:
        >>> merged = merge_adapter(peft_model)
    """
    raise NotImplementedError(
        "merge_adapter is not yet implemented. "
        "It will merge LoRA weights into the base model and return the merged model."
    )

# Rune

**Treating LoRA weight space as episodic memory for unbounded context in local coding agents**

---

## Vision

### The Context Window Problem

Current coding agents face a fundamental architectural constraint: as codebases grow, token-based context windows become bloated, expensive, and ultimately insufficient. Even with 400K-token windows, maintaining state across long coding sessions requires quadratic compute overhead and suffers from "needle in a haystack" retrieval failures. For local Small Language Models (SLMs) under 10B parameters, this limitation is existential.

Rune addresses this by **moving state representation from the token-level context window into the model's weight-space** via dynamic, task-specific LoRA adapters. Rather than stuffing ever-more tokens into context, we encode procedural knowledge—architecture specifications, execution feedback, coding conventions—directly into the model's parameters as modular, composable memory units.

### The Solution: Parametric Episodic Memory

Rune implements a **recursive code generation loop** where each iteration:

1. **Generates code** using the current adapter state
2. **Executes** in a sandboxed environment
3. **Distills feedback** (errors, test results, architectural constraints) into LoRA weight updates via a hypernetwork
4. **Composes memory** by merging task-specific adapters into a persistent library

**Initial Bootstrapping**: Before the first code generation iteration, the hypernetwork processes the project specification—goals, implementation rules, architecture conventions, and organizational structure—to create an initial project-specific adapter. This ensures the base model is immediately contextualized to the project's requirements, rather than starting from a generic coding baseline.

This transforms coding from a stateless LLM call into a **sequential decision-making process with parameter-space memory**. The agent doesn't just retrieve documentation—it internalizes procedural logic as trainable weight deltas, creating "skill-based memory" that persists across sessions.

**Core Innovation**: We treat the LoRA weight space as episodic memory. Each coding trajectory becomes a reusable adapter that captures not just what was done, but how to do it. By composing these adapters, the agent builds an unbounded, hierarchical memory structure that scales independently of context window size.

---

## Architecture Overview

### The Recursive Loop

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Base SLM (<10B params) + Active LoRA Adapter(s)       │
└──────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘
                   │
                   ▼
         ┌─────────────────┐
         │  Code Generation │
         └────────┬─────────┘
                  │
                  ▼
         ┌─────────────────┐
         │ Sandbox Execution│
         └────────┬─────────┘
                  │
                  ▼
    ┌────────────────────────────┐
    │  System-2 Reasoning Loop   │
    │  (Error Analysis + Logic   │
    │   Correction + Test Review)│
    └─────────┬──────────────────┘
              │
              ▼
    ┌──────────────────────────┐
    │  Doc-to-LoRA Hypernetwork│
    │  (Feedback → ΔW encoding)│
    └──────────┬───────────────┘
               │
               ▼
    ┌──────────────────────────┐
    │   Evolution Operator (E) │
    │  (Consolidate/Update/    │
    │   Forget/Merge Adapters) │
    └──────────┬───────────────┘
               │
               └──────► Update Active Adapters
                        │
                        └──► Persist to Adapter Library

### Key Components

**1. Doc-to-LoRA Hypernetwork**

A lightweight meta-learning network that maps execution traces to LoRA parameters, inspired by Sakana AI's Doc-to-LoRA architecture[44]:

- **Input**: Project documentation, architecture specs, error traces, test results
- **Output**: Rank-decomposed LoRA matrices (ΔW = BA, where B ∈ ℝ^(d×r), A ∈ ℝ^(r×d))
- **Architecture**: Perceiver-based encoder to handle variable-length inputs, chunk mechanism for contexts exceeding base model window
- **Training**: Meta-trained on diverse coding trajectories to learn the mapping from feedback → parameter updates

**Initial Pass Usage**: Unlike the original Doc-to-LoRA which focuses on knowledge updates, Rune extends this to project initialization. On the first invocation, the hypernetwork processes project specifications (goals, coding standards, architecture patterns, file organization) to generate an initial adapter that contextualizes the base model to the specific project domain before any code is generated.

**2. Evolution Operator**

Manages adapter lifecycle with three operations:

- **Consolidate**: Merge related adapters (e.g., "React hooks" + "useEffect error handling" → "React state management")
- **Update**: Refine existing adapters with new feedback
- **Forget**: Prune low-utility adapters to maintain global coherence
- **Conflict Resolution**: Detects architectural misalignments between adapters using structural graph analysis

**3. Dynamic Adapter Serving**

Built on S-LoRA principles for scalable multi-adapter hosting:

- **Unified Paging**: Shared memory pool for KV cache + adapter weights, reduces fragmentation

content.text:
I now have a deep understanding of Rune from reading all the docs. Let me synthesize what I know and what's missing for the E2E flow.

## What I understand

**Rune's core thesis**: LoRA adapters as episodic memory. Each coding session produces a trajectory → that trajectory gets distilled into a LoRA adapter → future sessions load relevant adapters → the model accumulates project knowledge in weight-space, not context-space.

**The full intended flow**:
1. **Project bootstrapping** — hypernetwork processes project spec → initial adapter contextualizes the model
2. **Task decomposition** — project broken into coding tasks with test suites
3. **Swarm execution** — agents run tasks through LangGraph loop (generate → execute → reflect → retry)
4. **Trajectory distillation** — successful trajectories → LoRA adapters (Phase 3: QLoRA fine-tuning, Phase 4: hypernetwork forward pass)
5. **Adapter feedback** — subsequent tasks load best adapters from registry → model improves
6. **Evolution** — merge/prune adapters based on fitness

**What exists (implemented)**:
- LangGraph agent loop (generate/execute/reflect/save_trajectory) ✅
- Inference providers (vLLM/Ollama) ✅
- Sandbox execution (subprocess/nsjail) ✅
- Trajectory recording + SFT formatting ✅
- QLoRA trainer (`train_qlora`, `train_and_register`) ✅
- Hypernetwork architecture (DocToLoraHypernetwork) ✅
- Adapter registry with lineage/fitness ✅
- Swarm orchestrator shell (swarm.py) ✅
- Evolution worker + TIES/DARE merging ✅

**What's missing for the E2E test**:
1. **Project decomposition** — no code exists to take a project description and break it into tasks with test suites
2. **Agent supervisor doesn't use LangGraph** — currently runs code directly, not through LLM
3. **No project bootstrapping step** — hypernetwork exists but nothing wires project spec → initial adapter
4. **Hardware constraint** — M4 Pro has no CUDA, so QLoRA training can't actually run. But Ollama inference works fine.

Let me think about what's feasible for a real test and design the plan.

<result>Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the Rune codebase. Let me provide a detailed analysis:

## Complete Rune Hypernetwork / Parametric Memory / Trajectory Pipeline Analysis

---

## 1. TRAJECTORY SYSTEM

**Location:** `/Users/noahdolevelixir/Code/rune/libs/model-training/src/model_training/trajectory.py`

**How Trajectories Are Recorded:**

- **Function:** `record_trajectory(session_id, steps, outcome, task_description, task_type, adapter_ids)`
- **Format:** JSON file stored at `~/.rune/trajectories/{session_id}.json` (or env var `RUNE_TRAJECTORY_DIR`)
- **Schema:**
  ```python
  {
    "session_id": str,
    "task_description": str,
    "task_type": str,
    "adapter_ids": list[str],
    "outcome": "success" | "exhausted" | None,
    "timestamp": ISO 8601,
    "steps": list[dict]  # Each step contains:
      {
        "generated_code": str,
        "stdout": str,
        "stderr": str,
        "exit_code": int,
        "tests_passed": bool
      }
  }
  ```

**How Trajectories Flow to Training:**

1. **Recording:** Agent loop (`services/rune-agent/src/rune_agent/nodes.py`) calls `record_trajectory()` in `save_trajectory_node()` after loop terminates
2. **Loading:** Training services call `load_trajectory(trajectory_id)` to retrieve the JSON
3. **Formatting for SFT:** `format_for_sft(trajectory)` extracts the final successful step (or latest step if exhausted) and returns 3-message format:
   ```python
   [
     {"role": "system", "content": "You are a Python code generator. Output only code, no explanation."},
     {"role": "user", "content": task_description},
     {"role": "assistant", "content": generated_code}  # from last successful step
   ]
   ```
4. **Empty on Failure:** If outcome != "success" or no step has tests_passed=True, returns empty list `[]`

**Complete Data Flow:**
- Agent loop records per-attempt: code → execution → (pass/fail)
- Complete trajectory persists to disk as JSON
- Training pipeline loads, formats, and consumes the SFT messages
- Adapter metadata stores `session_id` linking back to original trajectory

---

## 2. TRAINING PIPELINE

**Location:** `/Users/noahdolevelixir/Code/rune/libs/model-training/src/model_training/trainer.py`

### Path 1: Direct QLoRA Fine-Tuning (Phase 3 - Current)

**Function:** `train_qlora(session_id, adapter_id, output_dir, base_model_id, task_type, rank, alpha, epochs, learning_rate)`

**Architecture:**
1. Load trajectory JSON → format with `format_for_sft()` → create 3-message chat dataset
2. Load base model with NF4 quantization (4-bit)
3. Build LoRA config via `build_qlora_config()` targeting `q_proj, v_proj`
4. Use PEFT `SFTTrainer` (Supervised Fine-Tuning)
5. Save adapter as `{output_dir}/adapter_model.safetensors` + `adapter_config.json`

**Config Details:**
- Rank: 64 (default)
- Alpha: 128 (2x rank)
- Epochs: 3
- Learning rate: 2e-4
- Batch size: 1 (with gradient accumulation x4)
- bf16 precision
- Cosine learning rate scheduler

### Path 2: Integration with Registry

**Function:** `train_and_register(session_id, adapter_id, database_url, ...)`

**Flow:**
1. Call `train_qlora()` to produce weights
2. Compute SHA-256 hash of `adapter_model.safetensors`
3. Create `AdapterRecord` with:
   - id, version, task_type, base_model_id, rank, created_at
   - file_path, file_hash, file_size_bytes
   - source="qlora", session_id
4. Store in adapter registry via `AdapterRegistry.store(record)`

**All GPU imports deferred:** torch, datasets, transformers, trl, peft are imported inside function bodies (INFRA-05 pattern) so module is importable on CPU-only environments

---

## 3. HYPERNETWORK CONCEPT

**Location:** `/Users/noahdolevelixir/Code/rune/libs/model-training/src/model_training/hypernetwork.py`

**What is it:** DocToLoraHypernetwork — Perceiver-based instant LoRA adapter generation from token IDs (single forward pass, no gradient descent at inference time)

**Architecture:**

```
Input: token_ids (batch, seq_len) from trajectory text
  ↓
Lightweight token embedding (hypernetwork's own, not base model)
  ↓
Learnable latent array (num_latents=32, latent_dim=256)
  ↓
Cross-attention: latents attend over token embeddings
  ↓
Self-attention stack (4 layers) over latents
  ↓
Linear head: projects to all LoRA weight parameters
  ↓
Output: PEFT state_dict with lora_A, lora_B for each layer/module
```

**Key Parameters:**
- input_dim: vocab size
- num_latents: 32
- latent_dim: 256
- depth: 4 (self-attention layers)
- heads: 8
- rank: 8 (output LoRA rank)
- target_modules: ["q_proj", "v_proj"]
- num_layers: 28 (Qwen2.5-Coder-7B layers)
- hidden_dim: 4096

**Forward Pass Output:**
- Returns dict mapping `"base_model.model.model.layers.{i}.self_attn.{module}.lora_{A|B}.weight"` → tensor
- Each pair (lora_A, lora_B) has shape (rank, hidden_dim) and (hidden_dim, rank)

**Lazy Proxy Pattern:**
- `DocToLoraHypernetwork` is a lazy proxy that only builds the real `nn.Module` class on first instantiation
- Keeps module importable without torch

**Saving:** `save_hypernetwork_adapter(weights, output_dir, base_model_id)` writes:
- `adapter_model.safetensors` (weights)
- `adapter_config.json` (PEFT format config)

**Phase Status:**
- Phase 3: QLoRA direct fine-tuning (implemented, current)
- Phase 4: Hypernetwork forward pass replaces gradient descent (proposed, pending validation)

---

## 4. ADAPTER REGISTRY INTEGRATION

**Location:** `/Users/noahdolevelixir/Code/rune/libs/adapter-registry/src/adapter_registry/registry.py` + `models.py`

### AdapterRecord SQLModel
```python
class AdapterRecord(SQLModel, table=True):
    id: str (PK)
    version: int
    task_type: str (indexed)
    base_model_id: str
    rank: int
    created_at: str (ISO 8601)
    file_path: str (unique)
    file_hash: str (SHA-256)
    file_size_bytes: int
    pass_rate: Optional[float]
    fitness_score: Optional[float]
    source: str ("qlora", "hypernetwork")
    session_id: str
    is_archived: bool (soft delete)
    parent_ids: Optional[str] (JSON-encoded list)
    generation: int (evolutionary generation)
    training_task_hash: Optional[str] (dedup key, indexed)
    agent_id: Optional[str]
```

### Registry Methods

**Store:** `registry.store(record)` — Write-once, raises `AdapterAlreadyExistsError` if duplicate ID

**Query:** 
- `retrieve_by_id(adapter_id)` → single record
- `query_by_task_type(task_type)` → all adapters for a task
- `list_all()` → all non-archived adapters
- **`query_best_for_task(task_type, top_k=3)`** → top-k by fitness_score DESC
- `query_unevaluated(task_type=None)` → adapters with pass_rate IS NULL

**Lifecycle:**
- `update_fitness(adapter_id, pass_rate, fitness_score)` — update evaluation metrics
- `archive(adapter_id)` — soft delete
- `get_lineage(adapter_id)` — walk parent_ids chain

### How Adapters Flow Through Agent Loop

**Location:** `/Users/noahdolevelixir/Code/rune/services/rune-agent/src/rune_agent/nodes.py`

1. **Agent state intake:** `adapter_ids: list[str]` passed in `RuneState`
2. **Generate node:**
   ```python
   adapter_id = state["adapter_ids"][0] if state["adapter_ids"] else None
   result = await provider.generate(prompt, model, adapter_id=adapter_id)
   ```
3. **Provider selection:** `get_provider()` returns `VLLMProvider` (default) or `OllamaProvider`
4. **vLLM integration:** If adapter_id provided:
   - vLLM treats adapter_id as the model name in the request
   - Adapter must be pre-loaded via vLLM's `/v1/load_lora_adapter` endpoint
   - Generation applies adapter to base model

**Adapter Loading in Inference:** (`libs/inference/src/inference/vllm_provider.py`)
```python
async def load_adapter(adapter_id: str, adapter_path: str) -> None:
    # POST {base_url}/load_lora_adapter with {"lora_name": adapter_id, "lora_path": adapter_path}
    
async def generate(prompt, model, adapter_id=None, max_tokens=1024):
    effective_model = adapter_id if adapter_id else model  # Use adapter_id as model name
    response = await client.chat.completions.create(model=effective_model, ...)
```

---

## 5. TASK DECOMPOSITION

**Status: NOT IMPLEMENTED**

No existing code decomposes a project description into individual coding tasks. 

**Where it would fit:**
- Agent loop intake: currently expects single `task_description` and `test_suite` as input
- Swarm orchestrator (`scripts/swarm.py`): loads `task_source` (JSON file) with pre-defined tasks
- Demo projects (`scripts/demo_project.py`): provide monolithic task (entire FastAPI app as single problem)

**Current Task Format (from swarm.py):**
```python
{
    "task_id": str,
    "task_description": str,
    "test_suite": str,
    "task_type": str (optional, defaults to "function")
}
```

**What's Missing:**
- No LLM-based task planner that takes "Build a URL shortener" → ["Set up route handlers", "Implement storage", "Add validation", ...]
- No task dependency graph
- No hierarchical task structure
- No automatic test generation for subtasks

**Related Code Points:**
- `swarm.py:load_task_pool()` loads tasks from JSON but does not generate them
- `swarm.py:agent_supervisor()` processes pre-defined task list but does not decompose tasks
- No planning logic in the codebase

---

## 6. ADAPTER FITNESS & EVOLUTION

**Location:** `/Users/noahdolevelixir/Code/rune/scripts/swarm_evolution.py`

**Fitness Calculation:**
```python
phi(a) = alpha * pass_rate(a) + (1 - alpha) * generalization(a)
```
- Default alpha: 0.7 (prefer specialization)
- pass_rate: test-passing rate on held-out tasks
- generalization: cross-task coverage metric (TBD)
- Stored in `fitness_score` column

**Evolution Operations:**

1. **Evaluate:** `evaluate_fitness()` computes and updates fitness
2. **Merge (TIES-Merging):** For each task type with ≥5 adapters:
   - `query_best_for_task(task_type, top_k=3)` gets top 3
   - `ties_merge(state_dicts)` combines them (trim, elect sign, average)
   - Creates new adapter entry with `parent_ids` pointing to sources
3. **Prune:** Archive any adapter with `fitness_score < 0.3`
4. **Promote:** New merged adapters inherit `generation = max(parents) + 1`

**Merge Implementations:**
- **TIES-Merging:** Trim parameters below density threshold, majority-vote sign, average matching values
- **DARE-Merging:** Drop & rescale — randomly drops a fraction, rescales, averages

---

## 7. COMPLETE PROJECT→TASKS→SOLVE→TRAIN→IMPROVE LOOP

### What Exists

**Implemented (Phase 3):**
1. ✅ **Agent Loop:** Single task → generate/execute/reflect cycles → trajectory recorded
2. ✅ **Direct QLoRA Training:** Trajectory → SFT dataset → fine-tuned adapter
3. ✅ **Adapter Registry:** SQLite-backed metadata + safetensors storage, write-once policy
4. ✅ **Adapter Serving:** vLLM with LoRA hot-loading, OpenAI-compatible API
5. ✅ **Evolution:** Fitness-driven merge/prune/archive operations
6. ✅ **Swarm Execution:** Multiple agents, training queue, evolution worker coordinated via asyncio

**Proposed (Phase 4):**
7. 🔬 **Hypernetwork:** Single forward pass replaces gradient descent (not yet empirically validated)
8. 📋 **Task Decomposition:** LLM-based planning to break projects into subtasks (NOT IMPLEMENTED)

### What's Missing

**Critical Gap:**
- **No Project→Task Bridge:** System accepts pre-defined task list but cannot decompose a project description
  
  Example: 
  - Input: "Build a URL shortener REST API with persistence, rate limiting, and analytics"
  - Currently: Must manually specify individual tasks
  - Missing: Auto-planner that generates task list and test suites

**Process Flow as Currently Implemented:**
```
Task JSON → load_task_pool()
    ↓
agent_supervisor (N agents, M tasks)
    ↓
generate/execute/reflect loop
    ↓
record_trajectory()
    ↓
training_queue (via TrainingRequest)
    ↓
train_qlora() OR hypernetwork forward pass
    ↓
AdapterRegistry.store()
    ↓
evolution_sweep() (periodic merge/prune)
```

**What Would Complete the Loop:**
```
Project Description
    ↓
[LLM Task Planner] ← NOT IMPLEMENTED
    ↓
Task List + Test Suites
    ↓
[Rest of pipeline as above]
```

---

## 8. KEY FILES SUMMARY

| Component | File | Key Functions |
|-----------|------|---------------|
| **Trajectory** | `libs/model-training/src/model_training/trajectory.py` | `record_trajectory()`, `load_trajectory()`, `format_for_sft()` |
| **QLoRA Training** | `libs/model-training/src/model_training/trainer.py` | `train_qlora()`, `train_and_register()` |
| **Hypernetwork** | `libs/model-training/src/model_training/hypernetwork.py` | `DocToLoraHypernetwork`, `save_hypernetwork_adapter()` |
| **PEFT Utils** | `libs/model-training/src/model_training/peft_utils.py` | `build_qlora_config()`, `apply_lora_adapter()` |
| **Adapter Merging** | `libs/model-training/src/model_training/merging.py` | `ties_merge()`, `dare_merge()`, `load_adapter_state_dict()` |
| **Registry** | `libs/adapter-registry/src/adapter_registry/registry.py` | `AdapterRegistry` class + CRUD methods |
| **Registry Models** | `libs/adapter-registry/src/adapter_registry/models.py` | `AdapterRecord` SQLModel |
| **Agent State** | `services/rune-agent/src/rune_agent/state.py` | `RuneState` TypedDict |
| **Agent Nodes** | `services/rune-agent/src/rune_agent/nodes.py` | `generate_node()`, `execute_node()`, `reflect_node()`, `save_trajectory_node()` |
| **Inference Provider** | `libs/inference/src/inference/provider.py` | `InferenceProvider` ABC + `GenerationResult` |
| **vLLM Provider** | `libs/inference/src/inference/vllm_provider.py` | `VLLMProvider` with adapter loading |
| **Swarm Orchestrator** | `scripts/swarm.py` | `agent_supervisor()`, `run_swarm()`, `load_task_pool()` |
| **Training Workers** | `scripts/swarm_workers.py` | `training_pool_manager()`, `memory_watchdog()` |
| **Evolution** | `scripts/swarm_evolution.py` | `evolution_sweep()`, TIES-merge logic |
| **Training Service** | `services/training-svc/src/training_svc/routers/training.py` | `/train/lora`, `/train/hypernetwork` endpoints |

---

## 9. CRITICAL DESIGN DECISIONS

1. **Write-Once Adapter Storage:** No adapter is ever overwritten; new versions create separate entries with `parent_ids` tracking lineage
2. **Deferred GPU Imports:** All GPU-dependent code is inside function bodies (INFRA-05) so library is importable on CPU
3. **Single-GPU Time-Sharing:** Training pool coordinates with vLLM via sleep/wake API to share single GPU
4. **Fitness-Driven Evolution:** Empirical evaluation (pass_rate, generalization) before promoting adapters, not assumed safety via orthogonality
5. **Trajectory Persistence:** Complete trajectory (all attempts, all execution output) recorded for both successful and failed tasks
6. **SFT Format Conversion:** Only successful trajectories produce training data; failed trajectories recorded but discarded for training
7. **Lazy Hypernetwork Proxy:** Module imports work in CPU CI; torch only loaded on first instantiation

---

## Conclusion

The Rune codebase implements a complete **trajectory recording → training → registry → serving → evolution** pipeline with working hypernetwork infrastructure (Phase 3: QLoRA, Phase 4: hypernetwork proposed). The critical missing piece is **task decomposition**: taking a project description and autonomously breaking it into individual coding tasks with generated test suites. This would bridge from "here's a complex feature request" to the current task-list-in-JSON intake point.</result>

questions.question: On your M4 Pro there's no CUDA, so QLoRA training can't actually produce real adapters. How should we handle the training/distillation step in this E2E test?

options.label: Mock the training step (Recommended)
options.description: Save trajectories to disk as real JSON, log that training would happen, but skip actual QLoRA. This validates the full pipeline (decompose → swarm → LLM generate → sandbox execute → trajectory save) without needing GPU. The adapter feedback loop is exercised structurally but doesn't produce real weight updates.

options.label: Use hypernetwork on CPU
options.description: The DocToLoraHypernetwork is a small PyTorch model that can run on CPU. We could generate adapter weights via forward pass (random/untrained hypernetwork, so adapters won't be meaningful). This exercises more of the pipeline but adds complexity and the adapters would be noise.

options.label: Rent a GPU
options.description: Use a cloud GPU (Modal, Lambda, etc.) to run vLLM + QLoRA training for a real end-to-end test with actual adapter distillation.

questions.question: For the project decomposition step (breaking a project into tasks), how should the system generate the task breakdown?

options.label: LLM-generated (Recommended)
options.description: Add a 'planner' step that prompts the same Ollama model to decompose the project description into individual coding tasks with test suites. This is the natural approach — the model plans its own work. Risk: 1.5B model may struggle with test suite generation.

options.label: Pre-defined task pool
options.description: We manually write a tasks.json with well-defined tasks and tests for a sample project. Simpler, guaranteed quality test suites, focuses the E2E test on the swarm/loop/trajectory pipeline rather than the planning quality.

options.label: Hybrid: LLM plans, we validate
options.description: LLM generates the task breakdown, we review and fix any bad test suites before feeding to the swarm. Best of both worlds but more interactive.

options.description: Use PyTorch MPS backend on M4 to do direct LoRA fine-tuning on the 1.5B model. This builds the initial (trajectory, adapter) corpus needed to later train the hypernetwork. Slower but produces real adapters.

options.label: Train hypernetwork from scratch
options.description: Generate synthetic trajectories, create ground-truth adapters via fine-tuning, then train the hypernetwork on those pairs. Full pipeline but significant upfront work.

options.label: Use untrained hypernetwork
options.description: Run the hypernetwork with random weights to validate the pipeline architecture. Adapters will be noise but every component gets exercised end-to-end. We fix quality later.

options.description: mlx-lm natively supports LoRA on Apple Silicon. We'd add a new MLXProvider alongside vLLM/Ollama. This closes the feedback loop on M4 hardware.

options.label: Use Ollama, skip adapter loading
options.description: Run inference through Ollama, adapters get saved to registry but don't affect generation. Validates pipeline structure but not the feedback loop quality.

options.label: Use llama-cpp-python with LoRA
options.description: llama.cpp supports applying LoRA adapters on CPU/Metal. We'd add a new provider wrapping llama-cpp-python. Closes the feedback loop.
